findings.title: A custom --out path without lowercase .json is overwritten by the Markdown summary
findings.summary: score-audit derives the summary path with a case-sensitive replacement that leaves any non-.json output path unchanged. For --out scorecard, scorecard.JSON, or another suffix, writeTextFile writes Markdown onto the same path immediately after writeJsonFile, destroying the promised JSON scorecard while the command reports both artifacts as written.
evidence: src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:41 - --out is accepted as an arbitrary path and is not required or normalized to end in lowercase .json.
src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:88-91 - when the regex does not match, summaryPath === outPath, so the second write overwrites the first.

findings.title: Absorbed i-slot finding keeps scanning and can silently swallow later findings (finding loss in dedupe)
findings.summary: In both crossLensDedupe (audit draw: breakOnAbsorbedSurvivor:false, survivorMutation:'mutate' per src/audit/reporting/mergeFindings.ts:111-117) and sameLensDedupe, when the i-slot finding is absorbed (!keepA) the inner j-loop continues using the already-removed finding as a comparison basis; only removed.has(group[j]) is re-checked, never removed.has(group[i]). If the removed i-slot finding later wins a pair (higher sev/conf than a subsequent j'), absorbFinding merges j' into the removed ghost, j' is marked removed, and its content lands nowhere in the output — the finding is silently dropped from the audit report. The prior high finding stands; the mechanism is confirmed on the live audit path.

affected_files.quoted_text: if (policy.breakOnAbsorbedSurvivor && !keepA) break;
const [survivor, absorbed] = keepA ? [a, b] : [b, a];

evidence: src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts:244 - the inner-loop break is gated on policy.breakOnAbsorbedSurvivor; audit passes false (src/audit/reporting/mergeFindings.ts:117), so after !keepA the removed group[i] continues to be compared against later candidates.

src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts:190,308 - removed.has(group[i]) is only checked at the top of the OUTER loop; inside the j-loop only group[j] is checked, so an absorbed i-slot finding can become a 'survivor' for a later pair.

src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts:324-327 - sameLensDedupe has the identical structure with no break at all: j' absorbed into a removed finding is filtered out at line 331 while its merged evidence/files exist only on the removed ghost.

findings.title: Auto-resolution selects OpenCode for a launch mode it always rejects
findings.summary: The fresh-session factory auto-selects OpenCode when running inside OpenCode or when it is explicitly configured and available, but OpenCodeProvider rejects every headless launch before spawning. Audit and remediation worker launch paths use headless mode, so the advertised automatic OpenCode configuration deterministically strands all automated work instead of selecting a capable backend or falling back.

src/shared/providers/opencodeProvider.ts:47-54 - the selected provider rejects headless mode unconditionally before invoking its launcher.
src/audit/cli/rollingAuditDispatch.ts:263-271 and src/remediate/phases/workerTasks.ts:54-62 - both automated fresh-session consumers construct launch input with uiMode `headless`.

findings.title: Autonomous leftovers become approved when the gate is replayed
findings.summary: The autonomous branch intentionally persists leftovers with an empty declined list, but the decision replay path reconstructs approval by excluding only declined IDs and ignores approved_ids. On a later contract-pipeline step, every survivor, including non-allowlisted leftovers, is therefore returned as approved and can re-enter the active autonomous remediation run.
evidence: src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts:2778 - the first autonomous crossing computes a narrow approvedSet, stores approved_ids, deliberately stores declined as an empty array, and returns only the approved subset.

src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts:2837 - subsequent crossings load the durable record but ignore approved_ids; because autonomous records have declined: [], survivors.filter(!declinedIds.has(...)) approves the entire survivor set.

src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts:3053 - handleReadyIntakeContractPipeline is re-entered across contract-pipeline next-step calls until extracted-plan.json exists, so the replay path is reachable within the same run.

findings.title: Circular dependency chain breaks initialization guarantees
findings.summary: The module import graph contains multiple high-order cycles (8+ modules) centered on src/shared/index.ts → src/shared/config/resolveSessionConfig.ts → src/shared/providers/auditorSources.ts → src/shared/providers/providerPathGuard.ts → src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts → src/remediate/index.ts. The system claims 'language-neutral' and single-sourced architecture, but cycles imply unspecified initialization ordering: which module's exports are available when a dependent module is first imported is undefined. A lazy import or re-export could be valid, but circular dependencies also permit transitive initialization-order bugs where module A.fn() calls B.fn() which calls A.prop that may not yet exist. No import-order contract is documented; no circular-dependency enforcement gate exists at CI time (knip --production does not flag cycles). This leaves the system vulnerable to 'works in CI, fails in production' initialization bugs where order-of-first-import differs from test order.

findings.title: Claims are only released at merge: a worker-dead round strands 20-min claims and livelocks the next-step drain
findings.summary: Deepening verification of FLW-COR-003: the finding STANDS at high/high with live run-artifact evidence. claimRegistry.clear() runs only for tasks classified terminal by a merge; a dispatch round whose workers all die without writing results (observed: LiteLLM/NIM 429 on every packet, 2026-07-22 14:00-16:30Z) never merges those tasks, so their 20-minute lease claims stay live, every subsequent next-step's dispatch grants zero packets (peer-claim skip), and the advance drain re-selects audit_tasks_completed until maxTransitions(100) — verified in artifacts: ~40 empty run dirs per 20-min lease window, 571 run dirs total, task-claims.json held by a dead round's poolId. Scope narrows to two concrete gaps: (1) no claim-release path exists for a dead round other than lease expiry; (2) a zero-granted dispatch round does not halt the drain as a pause. Remediation belongs in both this file's terminal-classification (release-on-dead-round) and the drain's zero-grant handling.

`[merge-and-ingest] ${notDispatched.length} task(s) not dispatched this round${reasonSuffix}: ${notDispatched.join(", ")}\n`,
await claimRegistry.clear(terminalTaskIds);

findings.title: Clone-mode dedupe loses or resurrects findings across repeated merges
findings.summary: crossLensDedupe replaces a group slot with a clone but keys cloneOf and removed by object identity. On a third matching finding, the clone is treated as a new original; later absorbed evidence can be written to a clone that is not returned, and if that clone is itself absorbed the original source object remains in the final output. Remediation can therefore retain a supposedly absorbed finding, lose merged evidence, and emit stale merge-map references.

affected_files.quoted_text: findings: findings.filter((f) => !removed.has(f)).map((f) => cloneOf.get(f) ?? f),

evidence: src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts:223-235 - after group[i] or group[j] is replaced by a clone, a subsequent iteration looks up that clone in cloneOf, misses, and creates another clone rather than accumulating on the original's canonical clone.

src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts:237-250 - removed can contain the intermediate clone while the final filter examines only original input objects, and the final map returns the first clone associated with the original rather than a later clone.

findings.title: Clone-mode dedupe tests cover pairs only and miss repeated-survivor corruption
findings.summary: Every crossLensDedupe test supplies two findings, so clone mode is never forced to merge into an already-cloned survivor or later absorb that survivor into a stronger third finding. A three-finding test should assert one final survivor, the union of all evidence/files, and transitive canonical merge-map targets; it would expose COR-P1-002.

findings.title: Consolidate the wrapper/ install-hosts, io, legacy, and opencode modules shared between audit-code and remediate-code
findings.summary: The repo-root wrapper/ directory carries a second, much larger duplication surface than the already-flagged src/*/providers and scripts/*postinstall/*verify-hosts pairs: wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-install-hosts.mjs (1189 lines) and wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-install-hosts.mjs (1183 lines) differ by only ~370 diff lines (mostly the audit-code/remediate-code string substitution and marker constants) — the bulk of the host-installer logic (writeGeneratedBinary, marker-based file patching, install/uninstall flows) is copy-pasted verbatim. The same pattern repeats in wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-io.mjs vs wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-io.mjs (155 vs 145 lines), wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-legacy.mjs vs wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-legacy.mjs (125 lines each), and wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs vs wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs (281 vs 249 lines) — the opencode pair is a near line-for-line mirror differing only in the AUDIT_/REMEDIATE_ constant names, subcommand allowlists, and permission scope strings. Combined this is roughly 2500+ lines of near-duplicate wrapper code, dwarfing the already-reported provider-wrapper-factory-consolidation (~150 lines) and systemic-provider-quota-wrapper-duplication findings, which cover src/audit/providers and src/audit/quota, not the repo-root wrapper/ bootstrap CLI. Extract a parameterized wrapper-core factory (tool name, marker strings, subcommand allow/deny lists, edit-permission globs) analogous to the recommended provider-wrapper factory, so audit-code-wrapper-*.mjs and remediate-code-wrapper-*.mjs become thin config callers into one shared implementation. This is the single largest remaining duplication surface in the repo by line count.

findings.title: COR-001 confirmed: merge-results treats any non-failing subset as a complete run
findings.summary: The merger never compares accepted task IDs with pending-audit-tasks.json. An empty or partial task-results directory therefore writes an incomplete run-results.json and exits successfully whenever the files that happen to be present validate; COR-001 stands.

affected_files.quoted_text: const total = passing.length + failing.length;
process.exit(failing.length > 0 ? 1 : 0);

evidence: dispatch/merge-results.mjs:23-34 - pending-audit-tasks.json is loaded only into taskMap; no expected task count or expected-ID set is retained for a completion check.

dispatch/merge-results.mjs:86-110 - total is derived solely from encountered candidates and success depends solely on failing.length, so zero encountered results yields a successful empty merge and a partial valid subset also exits 0.

findings.title: COR-002 confirmed: every JSON support artifact is ingested as an audit result
findings.summary: The task-results directory contains audit_result.schema.json, finding.schema.json, and audit_task.schema.json alongside worker outputs, but the merger selects every .json file. Those schemas are parsed as candidate AuditResults, rejected, written as failed tasks, and force a non-zero merge even when all real worker results are valid; COR-002 stands.

affected_files.quoted_text: const files = readdirSync(taskResultsDir).filter(f => f.endsWith(".json"));

evidence: dispatch/merge-results.mjs:43 - discovery has no canonical result-filename predicate or exclusion for the three schema files that the worker packet explicitly places in task-results.
dispatch/merge-results.mjs:45-82 - every selected object is validated as a result and any invalid object is added to failing, causing failed-tasks.json output and a non-zero exit.

findings.title: COR-003 confirmed: results for unknown task IDs are validated without the active manifest
findings.summary: When a result task_id is absent from taskMap, merge-results deliberately passes null to validateResult. validateResult then supplies an empty task array to validateAuditResults, disabling the validator's unknown-task and task-metadata checks, so a well-formed result for an arbitrary task can enter run-results.json; COR-003 stands.
evidence: dispatch/merge-results.mjs:60-64 - an unknown task_id maps to null but is still passed through the same acceptance path.

dispatch/validate.mjs:9-11 - null becomes tasks=[], and validateAuditResults only reports an unknown task when its allTasks array is non-empty; identity, assigned-file, and line-count checks tied to the manifest are consequently skipped.

findings.title: COR-005 confirmed: an empty/partial triage resolution still closes the run with blocked items
findings.summary: Re-verified against current HEAD: the resolution-present branch of runTriagePhase only visits finding_ids listed in triage_resolution.json's items[]; an item that is omitted, or whose action is neither 'retry' nor 'ignore', stays status:'blocked'. When none of the visited items requested a retry, execution falls out of the `if (resolution) {...}` block with no post-loop 'any items still blocked?' check and reaches the function's unconditional closing return — unlike the sibling 'no resolution yet' branch, which explicitly guards the same transition with a `stillBlocked` check. The finding stands unpatched; original evidence and grounding are unchanged.

affected_files.quoted_text:
// "closing" status triggers runClosePhase in the orchestrator switch
  return { ...state, status: "closing" };

evidence: src/remediate/phases/triage.ts:226-258 - the for-loop over resolution.items only sets item.status for ids present with action 'retry' or 'ignore'; any other item (omitted, or an unrecognized/absent action) is left untouched at 'blocked'.
src/remediate/phases/triage.ts:260-268 - when requiresRetry is false, the `if (resolution) {...}` block has no return and no check of whether any item is still 'blocked'; control falls through.

src/remediate/phases/triage.ts:327-336 - the sibling no-resolution branch explicitly re-filters `blockedItems` for `status === 'blocked'` before deciding to close or open a human triage prompt; the resolution-present branch has no equivalent check.

src/remediate/phases/triage.ts:363-364 - the function's final statement is an unconditional `return {...state, status: 'closing'}`, reached whenever the resolution branch does not itself return earlier.

findings.title: COR-007 confirmed and narrowed: applyReviewResolution ignores plan_id, and both call sites emit a constant, non-run-unique plan_id anyway
findings.summary: Re-verified against current HEAD: applyReviewResolution still reads only resolution.disapproved_findings/disapproved_tiers and never compares resolution.plan_id to request.plan_id, so a stale review_resolution.json from a different run can still approve/decline the current run's findings. Deepened scope: both real call sites in src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts build the ReviewRequest with a fixed literal plan_id ('path-a-review' / 'path-b-review') rather than the actual RemediationState.plan.plan_id, so even a correct plan_id-equality check would not distinguish two different runs on the same path — the identifier itself carries no run identity yet. The original finding stands, and the fix must also make plan_id run-unique at the emit site before a correlation check would be meaningful.

const REVIEW_GATE_PLAN_ID_PATH_B = "path-b-review";

findings.title: Cycle detection folds non-import edge kinds into the import relation, corrupting the flagship 'dependency cycle' findings and the risk score they feed
findings.summary: Raised by the mathematician perspective and independently verified by the judge against source. `allGraphEdges()` (src/audit/extractors/graphSignals.ts:103-116) excludes only `routes` and the co_change bucket before feeding `detectCycles()`; `references` and `heuristics` edges are merged in even though a `repo-path-reference` edge is a resolved string-literal path mention, not a load-order dependency. Concretely verified: src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts imports only `zod` and `./stepContract.js`, yet the graph bundle carries a `repo-path-reference` edge from it to src/remediate/index.ts derived from a prose mention of 'src/remediate/' — and every reported 7-17 module 'high severity' cycle in the current run shares that single phantom edge as its only audit<->remediate bridge. A comment produced import-cycle findings annotated with initialization-order-bug prose that cannot apply to a non-import relation. This contaminates prioritization: src/audit/extractors/risk.ts (lines 142-149) adds `member_of_cycle` to risk_score from this same cycle set, and six top-risk units carry it. JUDGE NOTE: this finding overturns the prior verdict's critical 'Circular import chains' finding (old DR-001) — the 8-17 module cycles cited there ride on the same phantom edge; the real, verified layering residue is far narrower (see DR-013). Fix: restrict cycle detection to edge kinds with load-order semantics (imports, calls) via a `structuralImportEdges()` selector; route `references`/`heuristics` into separately-labeled signals.

findings.title: deriveAuditState never emits an obligation state of 'blocked', so AuditState.status can never become 'blocked'

The 'blocked' branch is dead: this status can never be reached, even though downstream code (cleanup.ts's resumable check, operatorHandoff.ts's BLOCKED_STATUS-gated fields) treats it as a real, reachable state.

findings.title: Dispatch result schema contract not enforced across audit/remediate paths
findings.summary: RollingDispatchResult (src/shared/dispatch/rollingDispatch.ts:106-168) is the shared contract for dispatch outcomes across both orchestrators. However, the schema is ONLY type-enforced at compile time; no runtime schema validation is performed on worker results before merging (audit ingest, remediate accept). If a worker (or a middleware) produces a result.outcome value like 'unknown_outcome' (not one of 'success'|'rate_limited'|'timeout'|'error'|'credit_exhausted'|'quota_unclassified'), the calling orchestrator will accept it without error. The 'outcome' field is then used in control-flow branches (handleResult in rolling dispatch, mergeImplementResults in remediate dispatch) that assume outcome is one of the known enum values — an unrecognized outcome would silently fall through to a default case or cause type narrowing to fail. Counterexample: audit-side dispatch-result ingest (ingestResultsCommand.ts) reads worker JSON from disk without deserializing through a Zod schema that validates outcome enum membership. A corrupted result.json with outcome='partial_failure' (a typo) would be accepted and cause undefined behavior downstream. The contract 'outcome must be an enum value' is implicit, never validated at ingestion.

findings.title: Dispatch system complexity is opaque and spans 20+ files with hidden interdependencies
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The dispatch orchestration involves selecting tier (deep/standard), computing fanout (packet count), fitting packets to budgets, building pools with quota gates, rendering prompts, tracking results across merges — each step in its own file (tierRouting, packetFilter, packetPrompt, quotaPool, paths, pausePersist). Understanding dispatch requires reading 5+ files sequentially. The backlog documents multiple dispatch-related bugs (double-grant on cost-rank, model-keyed delta collapse, exclusion-pattern scope issues) that persisted for laps because logic was opaque. This is a security-relevant system (quota enforcement, access control); it should be simpler to audit. Solution: create an explicit orchestrator function that sequences phases with visible data-threading, and document the sequence with a high-level diagram.

findings.title: File-lock contract does not guarantee concurrent reader sees written state
findings.summary: The file-lock implementation (src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts) guarantees exclusive write access to a protected path but does NOT contractually guarantee that a reader acquiring the lock after a writer releases it will see the writer's changes. The lock uses token-based validity checks (readFile/check token equality before unlink), but this is a LEASE-model lock, not a synchronization lock. Between a writer releasing the lock (releaseLock calls unlink) and a reader acquiring it, another concurrent process could have acquired-and-released the lock multiple times, or the filesystem could have buffered/delayed a prior write. The caller-supplied fn() in withFileLock (line 214-238) is responsible for the critical I/O (readJsonFile/writeJsonFile), but there is no contract stating 'if fn reads a file inside the lock, it will see writes from prior lock-holders.' Tests pass because single-process runs serialize I/O; multi-process concurrent runners (multiple IDEs, multiple agents) may observe stale reads. This is particularly hazardous for state.json mutation (remediate state store) and quota-state persistence (shared quota ledger) where stale reads cause divergent decisions.

findings.title: Hand-built ~400-line subprocess lifecycle engine reinvents execa
findings.summary: Raised by the novelty perspective; size verified (406 lines). src/shared/providers/spawnLoggedCommand.ts is a from-scratch process-supervision engine: backpressure-aware log streaming, partial-line buffering, a 30s heartbeat, timeout -> SIGTERM -> grace -> SIGKILL escalation, manual win32 `taskkill /T /F` tree reaping, and a hand-documented cmd.exe re-quoting workaround (`windowsVerbatimArguments`, 'os error 123'). Every one of these is a solved, regression-tested problem in `execa` (tree-kill-safe cleanup, escalating-signal timeout, correct Windows escaping, stream/line helpers). Eight provider modules construct their spawns through this one controller, so the bespoke state machine is load-bearing for the entire dispatch surface. Swapping the internals for execa keeps the external LaunchFreshSessionResult contract, deletes most of SpawnRunController's bookkeeping, and likely fixes latent edge cases (grandchild reaping via detached process groups) the bespoke version only approximates. Consistent with the project's own two-tier dependency policy: process lifecycle is a correctness-sensitive mechanism whose grammar the project does not fully own.

findings.title: Malformed-submission crash-loop still open on the remaining incoming-JSON gates
findings.summary: Raised by the adversary perspective; mechanics verified by the judge. Commits 7906c518 and beb5feab fixed the same defect class twice in one day (malformed incoming/*.json either silently destroyed or re-emitted forever) via a quarantine helper, but wired it only into the design-review and graph-enrichment call sites. The generic `runOmittableGate` engine (src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1063-1079) still drives charter_extraction, charter_delta, charter_clarification, systemic_challenge (plus synthesis_narrative and critical_flow_fallback) with no shape validation before `apply()`: verified that `unlink` runs only AFTER `apply()` resolves (lines 1071-1072), and `apply()` forwards into `runAuditStep` where `CharterSubmissionSchema.parse` / `CharterDeltaSubmissionSchema.parse` / `ClarificationAnswersSubmissionSchema.parse` / `SystemicChallengeSubmissionSchema.parse` run uncaught (src/audit/cli/auditStep.ts:391-406). A Zod failure therefore throws to the top-level catch, exits 1, and leaves the malformed file in place — the next next-step re-reads it and throws again: an unbounded crash-loop that blocks the entire obligation drain until a human hand-edits .audit-tools/audit/incoming/. Worse than either previously-fixed sibling because it halts the tool with an opaque stack. Fix the class, not the instance: generalize the quarantine helper into `runOmittableGate` itself (wrap `apply()` in try/catch, quarantine on schema failure) so every current and future gate inherits it.

findings.title: No provider fallback when primary pool is exhausted; run halts instead of degrading
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes; no current perspective contradicts it). When a provider hits 429 rate-limit or credit exhaustion, the quota subsystem detects it and surfaces a pause. But there is no fallback strategy: the run does NOT automatically route the next dispatch to a secondary provider (e.g., if Claude is exhausted, fall back to Codex or OpenAI-compatible). Instead, the run waits for the operator to manually re-enable the primary provider or switch pools. For a 24/7 autonomous pipeline (the stated vision), this is a critical gap. The infrastructure exists (CompositeQuotaSource, multiple apiPool instances), but the orchestrator does not USE it — dispatch is tied to a single provider per run. Add a provider-diversity strategy: declare a FALLBACK list per orchestrator, attempt primary, on exhaustion/unavailability route to secondary, and log the fallback decision.

findings.title: Obligation-executor mapping is implicit and fragile; no transition enforcer prevents invalid state moves
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The audit orchestrator relies on a critical invariant: every obligation id in the PRIORITY array must map to exactly one executor in EXECUTOR_REGISTRY, defended only by a module-load assertion; no query answers 'what obligations does executor X handle' without searching both sources, and the assertion validates existence/uniqueness, not mapping correctness. In remediate, state transitions are checked only at phase entry points — no centralized enforcer prevents invalid transitions (e.g., implementing -> pending), so phase-logic bugs or concurrent state edits could corrupt a run silently. Solution: (1) a single explicit obligation registry from which both PRIORITY and EXECUTOR_REGISTRY derive; (2) a runtime transition enforcer in RemediationState that validates every status update against the allowed-transition table and throws loudly on violations, codifying the state diagram in code.

findings.title: Parallelize independent verification steps in verify:checks
findings.summary: verify:checks runs 8 sequential steps (check:deadcode, check:doc-manifest, build, verify:hosts, verify:remediate-hosts, smoke:packaged-audit-code, smoke:packaged-remediate-code) via profile-run.mjs's sequential runner. Only `build` is a prerequisite; the others can run in parallel with build and each other (hosts and smokes are independent, checks are read-only). Parallelize via package.json `verify:checks` using npm's built-in script composition (e.g., concurrent npm-run or a parallel runner like concurrently) while preserving fail-fast. Cuts verify:checks wall time from ~N×step_time to build_time + max(check_times, host_times, smoke_times).

findings.title: remediate's 5,209-line nextStep.ts monolith hand-rolls five gates that duplicate audit's generic gate engine
findings.summary: Corroborated by three perspectives (short-attention-span, mathematician, and the prior verdict). src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts is 5,209 lines / 84 top-level functions (verified) — the largest source file in the repo, the highest duplicate-line-count (931 per the repo's own analyzer), and the file CLAUDE.md names as the state-machine authority. Beyond raw size, the mathematician perspective identified the specific mechanism: audit already owns the right abstraction — `runOmittableGate<TIncoming,TStepKind>()` in src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts, a descriptor-driven 'poll incoming -> apply+continue, else omit-or-pause' engine reused across four audit obligations — but it was never promoted to src/shared, so remediate reimplemented the identical single-fire host-pause gate shape at least five times (runReviewApprovalGate, runLeanLightReviewGate, runPlanAmbiguityGate, runPlanningReviewGate, runPhaseBoundaryGate) plus nine near-identical one-line path builders. This is the project's own 'one core, two draws' principle violated at its most-trafficked file. Fix in two moves: (1) promote the descriptor gate engine into src/shared and re-express remediate's five gates as descriptors; (2) decompose nextStep.ts along its own vocabulary (per-phase handler modules: intake, plan-clarification, review-gates, dispatch-driving, phase-boundary math) with nextStep.ts reduced to the decision dispatch it is named for. The audit orchestrator's nextStep.ts warrants the same treatment at smaller scale.

findings.title: RemediationState optional fields assumed non-null in decision paths
findings.summary: RemediationState (src/remediate/state/store.ts:17-98) defines several fields as optional (`plan?`, `items?`, `clarifications?`, `closing_plan?`, `started_at?`, `plan_coverage?`) per the RemediationState interface. However, the state-machine transitions assume these fields exist non-null at certain states: decideNextStep in nextStep.ts invokes methods like `items[id].status` (accessing items[]) without null-checks after transitioning to 'implementing' status; runClosePhase (close.ts) consumes `state.closing_plan` without verifying it exists. The transition to 'implementing' does not contractually guarantee `items` was initialized (only optional-field convention says it should be). A corrupted state.json that carries `status: 'implementing'` but omits `items` will cause an unhandled error at runtime, not caught by the schema validator (validateState only checks status). This violates INV-remediate-state-04 (atomicity+validation): the state must be schema-complete the moment it is persisted, never lazily-filled on read.

findings.title: Run-id encoding still produces invalid Windows filenames
findings.summary: The encoder preserves every alphanumeric run id verbatim, so reserved Windows device stems such as `CON`, `NUL`, or `COM1` become unusable names such as `CON.json`; it also applies no component-length bound. findings.title: Signal-terminated profiled step is recorded as success, silently defeating fail-fast
findings.summary: Deepening verification: the finding STANDS, narrowed to POSIX signal terminations. In runProfiledCommands, `result.status ?? (result.error ? 1 : 0)` maps a signal-killed child (spawnSync returns status=null, signal set, error undefined) to status 0 — the step is logged as success, no failure is thrown, and the loop proceeds to the next command. Since verify:checks routes its gate sub-steps through this runner, an OOM/SIGKILL'd gate step on Linux CI yields a false-green gate. result.signal is never inspected anywhere in the file. On win32 kills surface as nonzero exit codes, so the hole is POSIX-specific — narrow the affected scope but keep severity: this is a release-gate integrity defect.

affected_files.quoted_text: const status = result.status ?? (result.error ? 1 : 0);

evidence: scripts/shared/profile.mjs:75 - `const status = result.status ?? (result.error ? 1 : 0);` — when a child is terminated by a signal, spawnSync sets status=null and signal, but error stays undefined, so the expression yields 0 (success).
scripts/shared/profile.mjs:82-85 - failure is only raised for `status !== 0`, so the signal-killed step records success and the fail-fast chain continues to the next command.

scripts/shared/profile.mjs:13-15 - the module's own invariant claims 'a non-zero step throws ... exactly like the former && chain'; a shell && chain WOULD stop on a signal-killed step (shells report 128+N), so the runner is weaker than the contract it documents.

findings.title: State persistence across CLI commands is a fragile handoff mechanism with concurrent-access risks
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict; now additionally corroborated by the adversary perspective's lock-race and concurrency-accretion findings (DR-003, DR-014). The audit-code workflow persists state to disk across multiple commands with no strong consistency guarantees: a failed step leaves state in an ambiguous intermediate position; resuming after failure requires knowing which state file is authoritative (backlog documents multiple staleness + recovery incidents); concurrent runs have corrupted state before (concurrent-next-step wipe). The file lock's timeout/staleness windows are fragile under contention or slow filesystems. Mitigation direction: keep all state in memory during one orchestrator run, persist only at graceful pause boundaries and final artifacts — or, if cross-invocation persistence stays, adopt the fenced-write primitive from DR-003 everywhere state crosses process boundaries.

findings.title: File-lock steal protects the lock artifact, not the critical section it guards; late writer silently clobbers
findings.summary: Corroborated across three sources: the adversary perspective (race analysis), the novelty perspective (library adoption), and the prior verdict (lock fragility). `withFileLock` (src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts) treats any lock older than STALE_LOCK_MS (30s) as abandoned and lets a waiter steal it via the `.steal` sidecar — the steal itself is race-free, but nothing checks whether the ORIGINAL holder is still alive: there is no heartbeat/lease renewal on the lock (unlike ReservationLedger/ClaimRegistry, which have `withClaimHeartbeat`). A `mutate()` callback doing arbitrary async I/O can legitimately overrun 30s — `mergeImplementResultsIntoState` (src/remediate/steps/dispatch/marshal.ts) does per-block async contract reads inside `StateStore.mutate()`, realistic to overrun on a large run with Windows fs flakiness — after which a second process steals the lock and starts its own read-modify-write while the first is mid-flight; when the first finishes, `createLockedJsonStore.persist()` calls `writeJsonFile` unconditionally, a blind overwrite with no version/fence check, silently clobbering the second writer. Fix: fence writes with a version/token compare so a stolen-lock holder's late write is rejected, or heartbeat long-held locks / move I/O outside the lock. Additionally (novelty perspective, aligned with the project's own two-tier dependency policy that imported smol-toml/yaml for correctness-sensitive formats): cross-process locking is exactly the correctness-sensitive, not-fully-owned mechanism that policy says to import — evaluate `proper-lockfile` (mkdir-atomic, stale detection, retries) as the substrate rather than continuing to hand-verify the bespoke steal protocol, whose own comments record a prior double-hold bug in this exact mechanism.

findings.title: Live long-held file locks are stolen after 30s: no mtime heartbeat while a lock is held
findings.summary: Staleness is judged purely by the lock file's mtime at creation — the holder never refreshes it while the critical section runs. Any fn() under withFileLock that exceeds 30s (documented real cases: a network token refresh persisted under the lock per httpQuotaSource.ts's ClaudeOAuthQuotaSource contract; slow/contended Windows FS; a GC pause) has its lock legitimately classified stale and stolen by stealStaleLock while still executing — two holders then mutate the guarded quota-state/ledger file concurrently. The steal path itself is carefully serialized, but nothing protects a slow LIVE holder. The prior high finding stands; compounded by the non-atomic releaseLock (fileLock.ts:202-207) which can then delete the stealer's fresh lock.

src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:214-238 - withFileLock provides no renewal mechanism for fn() bodies that outlive STALE_LOCK_MS, yet callers wrap multi-command verify lifecycles in it (e.g. src/remediate/steps/rollingSession.ts:404 wraps acceptNodeWorktree — which runs test suites — in withFileLock).

findings.title: Nine-plus equal-weight provider backends, duplicated per-orchestrator, for a product whose primary path needs one
findings.summary: Corroborated by the pragmatist perspective and two prior-verdict findings (provider simplification + audit/remediate provider duplication). src/shared/providers/ implements and tests claude-code, codex, opencode, openai-compatible, subprocess-template, vscode-task, antigravity, agy, worker-command plus the claude-worker proxy class (~23 top-level files), mirrored again under src/audit/providers and src/remediate/providers with near-identical wrappers differing only in config (sessionConfigPath, slashCommand, skipPermissionsDefault). CLAUDE.md's own conversation-first framing means the dominant real path is whichever single host agent the user is already in; the everything-agnostic mandate treats every backend as equally load-bearing, multiplying the surface (auth, launch args, parsing quirks, quota headers) that must stay correct for backends with near-zero use. Recommended: tier providers explicitly — 1-2 first-class, always-tested backends the conversation-first flow exercises, the rest demoted to a thinner adapter contract — and collapse the per-orchestrator provider mirrors into shared parameterized factories ({orchestrator, sessionConfigPath, slashCommand, skipPermissionsDefault}), eliminating ~1500 lines of duplication so a provider fix lands once.

findings.title: Readability decay has no mechanical gate: cap module size and require inline glosses for opaque ids, like the dead-code gate
findings.summary: The short-attention-span perspective's root-cause synthesis, judged down from its proposed 'critical' (readability, not correctness, is at stake) but kept high because it is the highest-leverage single change for the file-size, glossary, and naming findings and aligns exactly with the project's own 'whatever can be enforced in tooling must be' principle. The repo mechanically gates dead code (knip in verify:release) and glossary existence (id-glossary.test.mjs) but nothing stops a file growing to 5,209 lines, and nothing enforces the glossary's own stated policy that opaque load-bearing ids (INV-*, CE-* — which docs/glossary-ids.md admits are not even globally unique — N-*, SEAM-*, FND-*) be inlined with a one-sentence gloss at first use; today a single comment in a 2,135-line file can require a multi-step external lookup to decode. Recommended: two release-gated checks in the dead-code gate's spirit — (1) a file/function size ceiling with an explicit reviewed allowlist for the handful of legitimately irreducible files; (2) a lint requiring every bare identifier from the glossary's prefix families to carry an inline one-sentence gloss at first use per file. Converts 'read the whole system's jargon before you can read any file' into 'the file tells you what you need.'

findings.title: The charter/systemic/design-review meta-stack layers 5+ LLM-judgment stages before the first real review task
findings.summary: Raised twice independently by the pragmatist perspective (as pipeline ceremony and as a self-referential case study). audit-code's PRIORITY array runs ~24 obligations before a line-level review task executes; the meta-review layers alone stack charter extraction -> charter delta -> design review (contract) -> design review (conceptual, itself a 5-perspective fan-out + judge) -> charter clarification -> systemic-challenge loop (looped until quiescent). Each layer has its own executor, artifact, staleness rule, and drain slot. Individually defensible, cumulatively unweighed: the docs never measure what fraction of a run's tokens/wall-clock goes to pre-review meta-stages versus the per-file review tasks that actually produce findings, and a maintainer debugging 'why didn't my finding show up' must hold 24 sequential concepts. Recommended change (kept as the pragmatist stated it): collapse the meta-review layers into the ordinary review pass as configurable depth rather than separate first-class pipeline stages, roughly halving the obligation count without losing the judgment; instrument the meta-vs-review cost ratio first so the cut is evidence-driven. This finding subsumes the prior verdict's dispatch-opacity concern's root cause at the pipeline level (see DR-010 for the dispatch-local version).

findings.title: (judge-added) Testing strategy has integration gaps; dispatch+remediate e2e scenarios underspecified
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (judge-added there; unaffected by the upstream changes, and sharpened by DR-017's evidence that test volume skews unit/meta). The project has extensive unit tests and per-step integration tests, but lacks e2e scenarios exercising full dispatch-and-remediate workflows: no test verifies a large parallel wave merges without loss; none exercises concurrent remediate runs on the same repo for state consistency under contention; none validates provider-fallback routing (DR-009) or a stale staleness-DAG entry being caught (DR-021). The system's riskiest properties (multi-phase orchestration, concurrent dispatch, state persistence under contention) are exactly the ones only e2e coverage exercises — rebalance test investment from convention-checking meta-tests toward a small suite of end-to-end workflow tests (audit a fixture repo, produce findings, remediate a subset, verify no state corruption).

findings.title: 58-file CLI surface with hand-rolled flag parsing across 19 files while commander sits adopted-but-unused on the audit side
findings.summary: Merged from the pragmatist and novelty perspectives plus the prior verdict's CLI finding. README frames the CLI as fallback with exactly two intended commands, yet src/audit/cli/ holds ~58 files (26 *Command.ts modules verified) each with its own argument handling, help text, and test file — a second product's worth of maintenance for a declared fallback. Compounding it, `commander` is already a dependency and remediate-code's CLI is built on it, but audit-code hand-rolls `getFlag`/`hasFlag` (src/audit/cli/args.ts, a linear argv scan with a next-token heuristic) copy-pasted across 19 command files — the one-core-two-draws principle violated at the CLI layer. Recommended: either prune the audit CLI to the entry points users/CI actually invoke (next-step, status, 2-3 diagnostics) folding the rest into internal library functions, or support it as a real product — and in either case consolidate audit-code onto commander to delete the bespoke parser and its 19 call sites of duplicated flag boilerplate, replacing the 25-branch switch with a queryable command registry.

findings.title: Artifact staleness DAG could cascade phantom work if dependency map drifts from reality
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The staleness engine marks artifacts stale based on the explicit dependency DAG. If a new artifact type is added without updating the DAG, its dependencies are invisible and stale upstreams never trigger regeneration (silent miss); if the DAG overstates a dependency, phantom staleness cascades downstream forcing expensive re-runs. There is no automatic consistency check between the artifact registry (ARTIFACT_DEFINITIONS) and the DAG — they can drift silently. Solution: load-time assertion that every artifact in ARTIFACT_DEFINITIONS has an entry in the DAG (even if empty), so a forgotten DAG entry fails loudly at module load.

findings.title: Auditor identity not isolated when concurrent runs share artifact directory
findings.summary: The system supports resumable, multi-step audit runs via persistent .audit-tools/audit/ artifact directories. However, there is no contract enforcing that only ONE auditor/agent can operate on a given artifact directory at a time. The setup expects 'one agent per run' but does not prevent two Claude Code instances or two agent processes from mounting the same .audit-tools/audit path and calling next-step concurrently. The remediate state store uses a file lock (state.lock) to serialize writes, but the audit-side advance orchestrator has no equivalent lock-before-write on the entire artifact tree. Counterexample: Agent A and Agent B both call audit-code next-step against the same repo/.audit-tools/audit directory simultaneously. Both read the current artifact bundle, both compute the next obligation, both dispatch work, and both attempt to write back. The last writer wins (atomic temp-then-rename), so Agent A's work is clobbered by Agent B's. The system does not declare 'artifact directory is exclusive to one auditor' as a written precondition, nor does it enforce it at entry (no acquire-lock-before-advance pattern). This means 'concurrent multi-IDE audits' would silently corrupt state, violating the durable-design principle 'enforce robustness in tooling, not host discretion.'

findings.title: Auto-resolution is uiMode-blind: headless dispatch inside an opencode session deterministically resolves to a provider whose launch always throws
findings.summary: chooseAutoProvider's top rung resolves 'opencode' whenever OPENCODE is set (and the config-gated rung whenever opencode is configured+available), with no awareness of the run's uiMode. OpenCodeProvider.launch then hard-fails any headless input by design (PB-1 guard). The result: a headless run inside an opencode session (or with configured opencode) resolves to a backend guaranteed to throw at launch even when a headless-capable provider (codex, openai-compatible) is configured and would have matched a lower rung. The failure is loud (good), but resolution selecting a known-unservable provider is a resolution-layer defect, not just a launch guard. Narrowed from high: the guard makes this fail-fast rather than hang, so impact is a blocked dispatch with an actionable error, not silent misbehavior.

findings.title: classifyFindingRisk's lensIsSafe regex can never match a real Finding.lens value
findings.summary: classifyFindingRisk classifies a finding as risk-tier "safe" via lensIsSafe when finding.lens matches /\b(style|format|lint|typo|whitespace|cosmetic|config)\b/, but Finding.lens is restricted to the fixed 11-value canonical enum (correctness, architecture, maintainability, security, reliability, performance, data_integrity, tests, operability, config_deployment, observability), none of which match that regex — "config_deployment" fails too because the underscore after "config" breaks the \b word boundary. The lensIsSafe branch is therefore dead for every real finding, silently narrowing the "safe" tier to the severity/confidence (lowRisk) path only, which in turn feeds classifyChangeKind/evaluateAutonomousFinding's autonomous auto-approval gate.

src/remediate/steps/stepUtils.ts:138-140 - the lensIsSafe==true branch (and its 'style/format/config lens' rationale string) is consequently unreachable code for any finding produced by this codebase's audit pipeline.

findings.title: Consolidate duplicated schema object definitions using JSON Schema references
findings.summary: schemas/audit_result.schema.json and schemas/audit_results.schema.json contain identical object-definition duplication: audit_results.schema.json duplicates the entire 550-line audit_result object structure inline within its array items, instead of referencing audit_result.schema.json via `$ref`. This creates a single-source-of-truth violation and doubles maintenance burden when the result schema evolves. Refactor audit_results.schema.json to use `items: { "$ref": "audit_result.schema.json" }` (requires audit_result.schema.json to move its root object definition into a `definitions` or `$defs` block, or use a relative $ref pattern compatible with schema tooling). Cuts duplication by ~550 lines, clarifies intent, eases future schema changes.

findings.title: derivePhaseCut does not colocate cyclic modules despite its own documented/emitted claim
findings.summary: derivePhaseCut's cycle handling returns 0 for a back-edge inside computeTier instead of grouping cycle members at one tier. Tracing the algorithm for a 2-node cycle A<->B (A sorts before B in the outer loop) gives tier(B)=1 and tier(A)=2 — two DIFFERENT tiers, not 'placed together' as the module's header doc and the PhaseCut.has_cycle doc comment both claim. renderPhaseCutSection then tells the design-critique reviewer 'its members were tiered together' even when, per this trace, they were not — the tier split is an artifact of module-name sort order / recursion order, not a principled grouping.

affected_files.quoted_text:
topologically tiered, so its members are placed together at the tier just past
 * their highest acyclic dependency (named-sorted, fail toward a LATER tier so a
 * cyclic module never front-runs a real foundation it transitively needs).
a dependency cycle was detected among the modules; its members were tiered together.

evidence: src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:60-100 - derivePhaseCut([{name:'A',depends_on:['B']},{name:'B',depends_on:['A']}]): the outer loop `for (const name of [...names].sort()) computeTier(name)` visits 'A' first; computeTier('A') recurses into computeTier('B'), which recurses back into computeTier('A') and hits the visiting-guard (returns 0). Unwinding: tier('B') = max(-1,0)+1 = 1, then tier('A') = max(-1,1)+1 = 2 — the two cycle members land in different tiers.
src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:41 - the PhaseCut.has_cycle doc comment itself asserts '(its members were tiered together)', which the traced behavior contradicts.

findings.title: Every failed/skipped 'binary' runner candidate gets two tool_statuses entries, contradicting the documented 'exactly one status per candidate' invariant
findings.summary: resolveBinaryCandidates() pushes an unresolvedStatuses entry for every 'binary' candidate that fails ecosystem-detection, admission, or download resolution. runAcquisitionEngine() (via runExternalAnalyzer) then unconditionally re-evaluates every registered candidate — including binary ones — and for that SAME failing candidate independently re-derives detect()/admitSpawn() (identical inputs, identical result) and, finding no entry in resolvedBinaries, emits its OWN status object with the same tool id and status ('skipped' or 'not_resolved'). acquisitionExecutor.ts concatenates both arrays (`[...unresolvedStatuses, ...statuses]`) as the marker's tool_statuses, so any binary candidate that fails to resolve is recorded twice, contradicting the code's own comment that the union is 'exactly one status per candidate (report-skipped-never-silently)'. A successfully-resolved binary candidate is unaffected (only one status).

findings.title: Hardcoded MAX_DRAIN_STEPS=64 is a guessed cap whose trip is indistinguishable from a legitimate pause, under a concept name that misleads
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict, extended with the short-attention-span perspective's naming observation. The obligation-drain loop caps at MAX_DRAIN_STEPS=64 per invocation — a proxy for 'how much work one host round-trip can handle' hardcoded without justification; too low adds round-trips (each paying dist-build check and state-reload overhead), and when the cap DOES trip it returns the same emit shape as a legitimate pause, silently masking a violated no-reopen invariant — thread a distinct 'capped' signal. Prefer a dynamic cap from remaining context budget. Additionally, the concept's public name ('one bounded step per invocation') actively teaches the wrong mental model — the implementation is a fold-aware drain with a soft cap plus a belt-and-braces hard cap that needs multiple comment blocks to justify; renaming to what it is (e.g. 'drain-to-pause') would let the name carry the correct 30-second model instead of three files of corrective prose.

findings.title: Learned quota limits persist across runs with no drift detection or reset path
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). Quota learning observes 429/524 errors and learns the actual rate limit dynamically, but a WRONG learned limit (too aggressive or too conservative) has no correction path mid-run: quota state persists in .audit-tools/.quota/ and carries forward across runs, so an incorrect learned limit sticks. A provider's actual quota can change (account upgrade) but the learned value never auto-resets, and manual edits get minimal validation. Solution: add a manual reset mechanism and a drift-detection trigger — if consecutive runs hit the same rate-limit error AFTER applying the learned limit, bump a stale-limit counter and eventually refresh from vendor headers or revert to defaults.

findings.title: next-step step-kind unions are hand-copied into two parallel Sets kept consistent only by this test, never checked against the real production union
findings.summary: seam-host-only-next-step.test.mjs hardcodes RETURN_KINDS_FROM_NEXT_STEP_HELPERS and CMD_NEXT_STEP_HANDLED_KINDS (plus ALL_STEP_KINDS and NON_TERMINAL_STEP_KINDS) as separate, manually-copied string lists mirroring the real StepKind union in nextStepHelpers.ts/cmdNextStep/steps.ts. The test's own comments admit this ('These are the kind values ... from the return type in nextStepHelpers.ts ... The test verifies the sets are consistent without importing the function body'): tests A1-A3 only prove the two hand-copied lists agree with EACH OTHER, not that either one matches the actual exported union or the real handler branches in cmdNextStep.ts. If a new step kind is added to the real code but the developer forgets to add it to both of these hardcoded Sets, the test still passes — the drift it claims to guard against can occur invisibly. This is the same duplicated-contract-kept-in-sync-by-convention smell as MNT-001, applied to the step-kind vocabulary instead of the artifact write-set.

findings.title: Obligation engine assumes all Obligation dependencies are satisfiable, no deadlock detection
findings.summary: The shared obligation engine (src/shared/engine/obligationEngine.ts) drives state transitions by selecting the next actionable Obligation (one with all its depends_on satisfied). The core loop calls findFirstActionableObligation, which iterates obligations and checks if all dependents have already transitioned to a terminal state. However, the contract 'a non-actionable obligation will eventually become actionable' is NEVER validated. If an obligation X depends on obligation Y, and Y's executor (the handler that transitions Y to a terminal state) never runs or always skips Y due to a condition, then X will remain forever non-actionable, and the engine's main loop will cyclically call findFirstActionableObligation, find nothing, and either halt (correctly) or enter an infinite loop (if the loop contract is 're-try forever'). Counterexample: audit-code's obligation cycle has 'provider_confirmation' → 'intention_checkpoint' → ... → 'results_synthesis'. If provider_confirmation is skipped (provider already confirmed, no obligation to fulfill), but intention_checkpoint depends on it, the system would either deadlock or incorrectly treat intention_checkpoint as blocked. The contract 'all dependencies in the ledger must have at least one executor path' is never validated at construction time (detectObligationCycle only checks for cycles, not for unreachable nodes).

Re-run the cycle report after DR-001's edge-kind fix to see whether any true import cycle remains.

findings.title: Path disposition logic is a cascade of 16+ conditionals; should be table-driven
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). src/audit/extractors/disposition.ts encodes file-path classification as nested if statements (isNodeModulesOrGit, isPackageManagerCachePath, isTmpPath, isBuildOutput, isVendorPath, ...), each calling a predicate from pathPatterns.ts. New patterns require new functions plus new conditionals, and priority order is invisible. The right abstraction is a table [{pattern, status, reason}] iterated in priority order returning first match — separating classification (one loop) from the pattern library (data), shrinking the file substantially and making the priority order explicit and testable.

findings.title: Provider-agnostic architecture violates by importing provider-specific enums in orchestrators
findings.summary: The shared module exports PROVIDER_NAMES (src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts line 277) as a hard-coded set of provider name strings. Both audit and remediate orchestrators import this enum and construct provider instances keyed by name (src/audit/providers/index.ts, src/remediate/providers/index.ts). This appears provider-agnostic until a new provider is added: the string must be added to PROVIDER_NAMES, the factory must be updated, and both orchestrators' provider/index.ts must conditionally construct it. The claim 'language-neutral' and 'provider-agnostic by default' (CLAUDE.md) is violated: adding a provider requires changes to 4 files (shared enum + two orchestrator factories). A truly provider-agnostic system would discover providers via configuration or plugin registration, not hard-coded enums. Counterexample: if a third-party wanted to add a custom provider backend at runtime (via environment variable or session config), the current architecture would refuse it — the provider name must be in the pre-compiled PROVIDER_NAMES set.

findings.title: referencedFiles contract assumes files on disk but doesn't validate before dispatch
findings.summary: LaunchFreshSessionInput.referencedFiles (src/shared/providers/types.ts:40-51) documents 'the authoritative set of files the worker is granted to READ' with 'a member that does not exist on disk is a to-be-created file and is NOT a failure.' This means a referenced_files entry can point to a nonexistent path, and the worker is expected to write it from scratch. However, the openai-compatible provider (src/shared/providers/openAiCompatibleProvider.ts) is documented to inline file CONTENTS for single-shot workers. The contract has a gap: what happens if a referenced file does not exist when the openai-compatible provider tries to inline it? A counterexample dispatch: remediate calls openai-compatible provider with referencedFiles=['new-file.ts'] (a file to be created). The provider's content-inlining code reads the file, gets ENOENT, and must either (1) skip it silently (losing the grant context), (2) inline an empty stub, or (3) fail. The current code is not clear which; the contract does not specify the behavior for nonexistent read-grants. This creates a silent contract violation: the worker is told it can READ new-file.ts (per the grant), but the content is unavailable because the file doesn't exist yet. A worker implementing `readFile('new-file.ts')` would work against an agentic CLI provider (claude-code, which has access to the real file) but fail against openai-compatible (which inlined nothing).

findings.title: Relative path candidates can resolve against the orchestrator cwd
findings.summary: A candidate containing a slash is checked with existsSync(command) before it is joined to the supplied audit root. If the same relative path exists in the process working directory, the runner selects that foreign executable and runs it with the target repository as cwd; path-like relative candidates should be resolved against root first.
evidence: src/audit/orchestrator/localCommands.ts:40 - every path-like command, including a relative one, is checked relative to process.cwd() by existsSync(command).
src/audit/orchestrator/localCommands.ts:91 - that cwd-relative match wins before the root-relative fallback at line 99.

findings.title: Release pipeline is a single 655-line, CC=158, non-resumable script chaining external network calls
findings.summary: Merged from the prior verdict (procedural complexity) and the adversary perspective (non-resumability), which sharpens the impact: main() runs a strictly sequential, non-idempotent chain — pre-tag gate -> version bump + local tag -> push commit -> push tag + gh release create -> poll CI -> poll npm — with no checkpoint file. Any failure between the local tag and completion (network blip, gh auth expiry, GitHub 5xx) leaves a state a bare re-run cannot recover: npm version double-bumps and git tag -a fails on the surviving local tag; recovery is manual git/tag archaeology (the project's own memory already documents a recovery procedure, confirming recurrence). Fix both axes together: decompose into per-step functions with error handling baked in (bumpVersion, commitTag, pushTags, createRelease, waitForCiRun, waitForNpm) sequenced by a thin orchestrator, and persist an on-disk phase marker so a re-invocation detects and resumes/repairs a partial prior run instead of re-deriving intent from git state by hand.

findings.title: releaseLock read-then-unlink is non-atomic: can delete another holder's freshly stolen lock
findings.summary: releaseLock reads the lock content, compares it to the owner token, then unlinks in a separate step. If the holder's own lock has gone stale (>30s hold) and a concurrent acquirer steals it and creates a FRESH lock between the read (which matched our token) and the unlink, releaseLock deletes the new holder's lock — the exact two-step pattern stealStaleLock's comment documents as admitting a double-hold, fixed for the steal path but retained verbatim here.

affected_files.quoted_text: const content = await readFile(lockPath, "utf8");

evidence: src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:202-207 - read, compare, then unlink as three separate awaited steps with no exclusivity between them.

src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:66-82 - the stealStaleLock comment describes precisely this read-then-unlink race class and serializes the steal path through a sidecar claim; releaseLock got no equivalent protection.

findings.title: Remediate wrapper module family duplicates the audit wrapper family wholesale (twin of MNT-W1-001, seen from this side)
findings.summary: All five remediate wrapper modules are structural clones of their audit counterparts, parameterized only by tool name, INSTALL_DIR, marker strings, and permission constants. The 1183-line install-hosts module repeats the entire install/verify/ensure/refresh machinery; -io, -legacy, -renderers, and -opencode repeat their counterparts' algorithms. Existing drift proves the convention-based sync is failing: audit-opencode defines AUDIT_CODE_DENIED_SUBCOMMANDS with deny rules and deny assertions while remediate-opencode has no deny machinery at all beyond 'rm *', and the verifyHostsIsolated local is named isolatedRepoRoot here but repoRoot (shadowing the module constant) in the audit twin. A shared parameterized core with per-tool config would collapse the N-places edit cost.

findings.title: retainAutoExclusions include-matching uses the retired bare pattern grammar, so an operator opt-in never lifts an axis-explicit auto-exclusion
findings.summary: retainAutoExclusions decides whether an operator `include` addresses a gate-authored auto-exclusion by testing `pattern === provider || pattern.startsWith(provider + ':')`, which is the pre-stage-4 bare grammar (`codex`, `codex:model`). Gate-authored auto-exclusions are always axis-explicit (`transport:codex/...` via exclusionPattern, `service:.../...` via serviceExclusionPattern), so neither test ever matches: `'transport:codex' === 'codex'` is false and `'transport:codex'.startsWith('codex:')` is false. The documented contract ('the operator named that provider in include — an explicit opt-back-IN' supersedes the auto-exclusion) is therefore never honored: the auto-exclusion is retained on every promotion, and since resolveDispatchExclusion merges auto_exclude unconditionally (include only lifts the self-spawn fold), the opted-in backend stays excluded at dispatch indefinitely. Fail-closed direction, but it silently discards an explicit operator decision — the exact silence class the module's own docs prohibit.
evidence: src/shared/providers/sharedProviderConfirmation.ts:1636-1640 - the `addressed` predicate matches only bare `provider` or `provider:`-prefixed patterns against `include` entries (plain provider names).

src/shared/providers/identity.ts:122-143 - exclusionPattern/serviceExclusionPattern (the producers of NewlyReachableBackend.exclusion_pattern used by autonomous fail-closed writes) always emit `transport:`/`service:`-prefixed patterns, which the predicate can never match.

src/shared/providers/sharedProviderConfirmation.ts:736-742 - resolveDispatchExclusion merges `policy.auto_exclude` into the rule set unconditionally; `include` only filters the local self-spawn-blocked list, so a retained auto-exclusion keeps excluding the opted-in backend at dispatch.

findings.title: Review-decision consumption is implemented twice
findings.summary: The intake review gate and planning review gate separately rebuild the request, read and apply the resolution, construct the same versioned decision record, write it, and archive the same input pair. findings.title: Rotated OAuth credentials file rewritten without restrictive file permissions
findings.summary: writeCredentialsAtomic writes the refreshed ~/.claude/.credentials.json (containing the live OAuth access token AND refresh token) via writeFileSync with no mode argument, then renames over the original. On POSIX the temp file is created with the default umask-derived mode (typically 0644), so a refresh rotation replaces the Claude CLI's 0600-protected credential file with a world-readable one holding both tokens.
evidence: src/shared/quota/claudeOAuthQuotaSource.ts:295 - writeFileSync(tmp, ...) passes no { mode: 0o600 } option, so the temp file (and therefore the renamed credentials file) inherits the process umask default rather than owner-only permissions.
src/shared/quota/claudeOAuthQuotaSource.ts:248-255 - the rotated claudeAiOauth block persisted here contains accessToken and refreshToken, i.e. long-lived account secrets.

findings.title: Run external analyzer candidates concurrently instead of one spawnSync at a time
findings.summary: runAcquisitionEngine (src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts) drives every registered ExternalAnalyzerCandidate (gitleaks, semgrep, eslint, knip, jscpd, osv-scanner, clippy, rubocop, hadolint, actionlint, type-coverage — 11 candidates in src/audit/extractors/analyzers/candidates.ts) through a plain `for` loop calling runExternalAnalyzer synchronously for each. runExternalAnalyzer's runSafetyGate does a blocking capability probe (`run(runnerProbeArgv(...))`) and then the tool spawn itself both go through runTracked (src/shared/tooling/exec.ts), which is built on Node's synchronous `spawnSync` — so each candidate fully blocks the process (npx/pipx/cargo acquisition + tool execution) before the next one even starts its probe. Each candidate is independent: separate runner (npx/pipx/cargo/bundle/binary), separate argv, separate output (own report file or stdout), no shared mutable state, and the own-vs-acquire/consent-gating logic (admitSpawn, run-safety gate) is evaluated per-candidate with no cross-candidate dependency. This is the same 'isolated child processes waited on one at a time' shape already flagged for the two postinstall sub-deployers (postinstall-sequential-spawn-serialization), but in a functionally different subsystem — the on-demand external-analyzer acquisition engine that runs during audit extraction, not install — and with a larger fan-out (up to 11 tools vs. 2 scripts), so a polyglot repo triggering several ecosystems (Node+Python+Rust+Ruby+Docker+GitHub Actions) pays the full sum of every tool's acquire+run latency serially. Switching runExternalAnalyzer's tool-spawn step to the async `spawn`/`execFile` equivalent (keeping the synchronous probe or making it async too) and running the per-candidate work through `Promise.all`/a small concurrency pool would collapse total acquisition wall time from sum(tool_times) to max(tool_times), directly speeding up every audit run that touches more than one ecosystem.

findings.title: seam-host-only-next-step exhaustiveness check compares two hand-duplicated literal sets, not the real source
findings.summary: The file's docstring claims 'This test fails whenever either side diverges from the shared interface', but RETURN_KINDS_FROM_NEXT_STEP_HELPERS and CMD_NEXT_STEP_HANDLED_KINDS are both hand-maintained literal Set()s declared inline in the test file (per its own comment: 'without importing the function body'). Tests A1/A2/A3 only check the two literal arrays against each other, never against the real return-type union in nextStepHelpers.ts or the real if/else branches in cmdNextStep. If the two production files actually diverge (a new kind added to one but not the other), this test cannot detect it -- it only detects if the test author forgot to keep the two copies in this file synced with each other. The real fix is the duplication itself: derive both sets from the production source (e.g. import the literal union type via a runtime-reachable const array, or grep/parse nextStepCommand.ts's branches) so the guard is unnecessary and the test exercises the real seam it claims to protect.

findings.title: Space-delimited pair keys corrupt valid paths containing spaces
findings.summary: Both coupling extractors serialize a pair as `a + space + b` and later split at the first space. Because repository paths may legally contain spaces, a pair such as `src/my file.ts` and `src/z.ts` is reconstructed as the nonexistent endpoints `src/my` and `file.ts src/z.ts`.

const idx = key.indexOf(" ");

src/audit/extractors/dataStateCoupling.ts:84-97 - the graph-derived coupling implementation repeats the same lossy encoding and decoding.

findings.title: Staleness dependency DAG not enforced at refresh time
findings.summary: The audit system (CLAUDE.md) documents 'Artifacts are continuity; dependency DAG is truth' and defines explicit staleness ordering in spec/audit/dependency-map.md and src/audit/orchestrator/staleness.ts. However, no runtime validation exists to ensure that when a downstream artifact is refreshed, all upstream dependencies are already valid (not stale). The rule 'upstream-valid before downstream-refresh' is a precondition for correct audits, but it is enforced only by convention in the orchestrator's obligation ordering. Counterexample: repo_manifest depends on file_inventory. If a developer manually deletes file_inventory.json from .audit-tools/audit/, then calls next-step, the obligation engine should refuse to refresh repo_manifest (it is stale). However, if the obligation order happens to queue 'repo_manifest_extraction' before 'file_inventory_extraction' due to a typo or reordering, the orchestrator will compute an invalid repo_manifest against stale inventory data. The PRIORITY array in nextStep.ts single-sources the ordering, but there is no compile-time or runtime assertion that the order respects the dependency DAG. A corrupted session could manifest dependency violations that silently propagate through subsequent analyses.
evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

findings.title: Stray worktree detection is reactive, not preventive; accepts work from the wrong tree
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The remediate dispatch system detects stray worktrees AFTER editing: when a node claims an edit but the designated worktree has zero commits, strayWorktreeSuspected is flagged in AcceptNodeWorktreeResult — by which point the risk has materialized (the agent edited in a tree the dispatch system didn't control). Prevention is the correct shape: verify before/at dispatch that the worker's effective worktree IS the designated one (compare identity markers), so a second spawned worktree fails fast instead of producing unmergeable work.

findings.title: Syntax-resolution executor accumulates tsc/eslint findings forever; a fixed issue is never dropped from external_analyzer_results
findings.summary: runSyntaxResolutionExecutor reads its own prior run's results and concatenates the freshly-detected items onto them before deduping only on exact path:line_start:rule:summary keys. tsc/eslint each report the FULL current set of errors on every run (not a delta), so an issue that no longer reproduces (already fixed) is never removed — it stays in the merged/deduped array and keeps being reported as if still present.
evidence: src/audit/orchestrator/syntaxResolutionExecutor.ts:283-287 - each run unions this executor's own prior results with the freshly-detected 'items' (merged = [...existing, ...items]) rather than replacing them with the fresh scan.

src/audit/orchestrator/syntaxResolutionExecutor.ts:289-298 - dedup only removes exact-match duplicates (same path/line_start/rule/summary); it never removes an 'existing' entry whose issue no longer appears in the fresh tsc/eslint output, so a fixed lint/type error is carried forward indefinitely.

findings.title: Three copies of the win32 cmd shim-quoting contract: shared exec.ts, wrapper-lib (test-pinned), and a weaker unguarded copy in build.mjs
findings.summary: wrapper-lib's quoteForCmd/resolveSpawn is a documented byte-mirror of src/shared/tooling/exec.ts, held in sync by a parity test — the sync test is the workaround for the missing single source. Worse, build.mjs contains a THIRD independent resolveSpawn whose quoting only doubles quotes: it neither caret-escapes the cmd metacharacters (& | < > ^) nor rejects '%', i.e. it silently lacks the CVE-2024-27980 hardening the other two copies encode. It happens to be safe today only because its arguments are the constants ['run','build']. One .mjs implementation importable by wrapper, build, and (re-exported) shared code would remove both the parity test and the weak copy.

findings.title: Trigger path filters omit paths the suite directly tests — wrapper/, committed host assets, and examples/ changes skip CI
findings.summary: The push/pull_request paths lists cover src/tests/schemas/scripts/etc. but omit several trees the vitest suite imports or inspects: wrapper/** (tests/audit/a7.test.mjs and host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs import wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-install-hosts.mjs and the renderers), the committed host assets guarded by the no-drift tests (.gemini/**, .github/agents/**, .github/prompts/**, .agent/**, AGENTS.md, opencode.json), and examples/** (audit-tests-invariants requires example files to exist). A push touching only those paths never runs the suite, so exactly the drift these guards exist to catch can land on main unflagged and detonate on the next unrelated commit. ci.yml states the governing rule in its own paths comment — 'A gate's trigger paths must cover every path the gate INSPECTS' (with a 2026-07-19 docs/** incident) — and this workflow violates it; ci.yml itself shares the same omissions.

findings.title: Adapter pattern for external tools lacks a shared extraction template
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). Each external analyzer adapter (eslint.ts, clippy.ts, semgrep.ts, ...) re-implements the same template: extract results -> map severity -> normalize -> call normalizeGenericExternalResults. A parameterizable adapter factory — AdapterFactory(input, fieldMap {filePath, severity, line, message}, severityMapper) — could handle most cases and reduce each adapter to configuration, so new acquired tools are added by providing config rather than writing adapter code. Lower priority than the structural findings, but a real composability gap in the own-vs-acquire pipeline.

findings.title: Admitted broker repair slot is never completed/released when the patcher throws
findings.summary: brokeredPatcher offers a slot to broker.broker() and, once admitted, awaits input.patcher before calling broker.awaitNextCompletion. There is no try/finally: if the patcher (an LLM call) rejects or times out, the exception propagates out of the brokered patcher with the admitted slot's reservation never completed, so the broker's budget/lease accounting for that slot is stranded — subsequent repairs against the same pool see less admissible budget than actually exists.
evidence: src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:171-176 - broker.broker() admits the slot (reserving against the repair budget) before the patcher runs.

src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:180-184 - awaitNextCompletion (the completion half of the reservation) is only reached on the patcher's success path; a thrown patcher leaves the admitted slot unreconciled.

findings.title: auditRegisteredHostFlags() proves only that a string occurs in the file, not that a flag is registered
findings.summary: Unlike remediateRegisteredHostFlags(), which introspects the live commander program's registered options, auditRegisteredHostFlags() regex-scans raw source text for the flag string. A match inside a comment or an unused string constant would satisfy the assertion the same as a real .option() registration, and a genuine registration built via a template literal or shared array (a syntax the regex doesn't anticipate) would go undetected - the two halves of the same parity check use materially different-strength techniques.

findings.title: buildWorkerRepairContract's validation semantics contradict its documented contract: every zod issue is marked required, including optional-field errors
findings.summary: The function's JSDoc states that errors on a field that is optional() in the schema map to required: false so O3's stage-1 coercion may clear them by dropping the optional sub-object. The implementation unconditionally sets required: true for every zod issue (a present-but-malformed optional field DOES produce zod issues). Consequence: a payload whose only defect is a malformed optional field is escalation-gated to the bounded LLM patch / re-dispatch path instead of being deterministically repaired by dropping the optional sub-object — the opposite of the documented degrade order at this validation boundary.

findings.title: Complexity/duplication analyzer is a JS/TS-only regex heuristic despite an in-repo multi-language parser and available acquired tools
findings.summary: Raised by the novelty perspective. src/audit/extractors/analyzers/complexityDuplication.ts computes 'cyclomatic-approx' by regex-counting branch keywords and detects duplication by hashing normalized lines, gated to JS/TS paths only — silently returning no signal for every other language. This sits against two facts already true in the repo: web-tree-sitter + tree-sitter-wasms are already dependencies used for real multi-language grammars (treeSitter.ts), and duplication detection has a purpose-built multi-language acquired tool (jscpd) that fits the project's documented own-vs-acquire policy of normalizing ecosystem-native analyzers into leads. As is, the `high_complexity` and `duplicated_code` risk signals feeding risk scoring are silently absent for non-JS/TS repos — the opposite of the language-neutral-by-contract principle, and a silent-degradation shape the project elsewhere treats as a bug. Either extend via the existing tree-sitter infrastructure or acquire jscpd through the adapter path.
