findings.title: Concurrency correctness is accreting as per-incident patches, not one verified model
findings.summary: Raised by the adversary perspective. The most concurrency-critical files (fileLock.ts, worktreeLifecycle.ts, reservationLedger.ts, claimLease.ts) are dense with individually-reasoned invariant comments (INV-WTS-1..8, CE-001/003/004, OD1/OD3, the steal-claim rewrite documenting the prior double-hold race). Each is correct for the race it was written against, but the pattern is reactive: an interleaving is observed in production, a narrow fix is documented in place, and the next unanticipated interleaving surfaces elsewhere in the same family. DR-003 (lock staleness vs critical-section duration) is itself a plausible race the existing invariants do not cover. As the project commits to more concurrent writers joining one run, the surface for new interleavings grows faster than one-bug-at-a-time patching. Recommended: consolidate the cross-process state-mutation paths (state.json, session-config.json, quota ledgers) onto one audited primitive — e.g. compare-and-swap writes keyed on a monotonic version — verified once and reused, rather than special-casing each new file needing cross-process safety.

findings.title: Every parallel-dispatch worktree shares one real node_modules via junction
findings.summary: Raised by the adversary perspective. `ensureWorktreeNodeModules` (src/remediate/steps/dispatch/worktreeLifecycle.ts) links each per-node worktree's node_modules to the MAIN checkout's via junction/symlink. Parallel dispatch over overlapping files is the stated goal, but every concurrent node's verify/test/build invocation executes against the same physical node_modules: shared .bin shims, shared tool caches (node_modules/.cache, .vite), shared resolution state. One worker running npm install/ci — or any build tool writing into node_modules as a side effect — mutates state every sibling's concurrent verify depends on mid-flight, a shared-mutable-resource hazard that scales with exactly the concurrency dimension the architecture is trying to increase. The in-code comment acknowledges cross-package resolution but not concurrent-write corruption. Options: read-only link/overlay, explicitly forbidding node_modules mutation inside worker targeted_commands with violation detection, or per-worktree installs for isolation-sensitive nodes.
evidence: runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

findings.title: First-party User-Agent strings duplicated in openCodeQuotaSource, kept consistent by comment convention
findings.summary: ROUTE_USER_AGENT hardcodes the per-provider first-party UA strings with the comment 'match the standalone sources' defaults' — the same literals each standalone source (claudeOAuth/codex/copilot) declares as its own default. The invariant 'these must match' is enforced only by convention: changing a standalone source's UA (e.g. because the anthropic endpoint tightens UA-based rate-limiting, which the comment itself says it does aggressively) silently leaves the opencode-broker route on the stale string. Single-source each default as an export of its source module and import it here.

affected_files.quoted_text: anthropic: "claude-cli (external, cli)",

evidence: src/shared/quota/openCodeQuotaSource.ts:32-37 - three UA literals with a comment promising they match the standalone sources' defaults; nothing mechanical ties them together, the exact every-edit-in-N-places change-cost tell.

findings.title: Global OpenCode bash table in remediate postinstall is stale: allows retired document-phase commands and omits accept-node
findings.summary: OPENCODE_REMEDIATE_BASH_PERMISSION in scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs still allows the document-phase commands (prepare-document-dispatch, merge-document-results) — a phase the project record says was dissolved (N-R13) — and does NOT allow 'remediate-code accept-node*', which is a live workflow command: the project-scope table in wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs seeds it and assertOpenCodeRemediatePermissionConfig requires it. So the globally-seeded config prompts ('ask') on accept-node while granting rules for commands that no longer exist. The two managed tables for the same tool have drifted.

affected_files.quoted_text:
"remediate-code prepare-document-dispatch*": "allow",
  "remediate-code merge-document-results*": "allow",

'ensure*',
  'next-step*',
  'prepare-implement-dispatch*',
  'merge-implement-results*',
  'accept-node*',
  'validate*',

evidence: scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs:44-67 - the global managed table lists prepare-document-dispatch*/merge-document-results*/validate-artifacts* and contains no accept-node entry.
wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs:29-36 - the project-scope managed table lists accept-node* and no document-phase commands.

wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs:137-152 - assertOpenCodeRemediatePermissionConfig requires 'remediate-code accept-node*' to be 'allow', confirming accept-node is a current, required workflow command that the global seed omits.

findings.title: Hidden coupling: src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts ↔ src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts
findings.summary: src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts and src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts repeatedly change together (Files changed together in 11 commit(s) (temporal coupling).) but have no import/call/reference edge between them. findings.title: Host capability handshake (C1) persists unvalidated JSON without schema
findings.summary: RemediationState.host_capabilities (src/remediate/state/store.ts:55, defined as `HostCapabilities | undefined`) is intentionally 'opaque to the state store: stored and round-tripped verbatim, never interpreted here.' However, validateState (line 127-142) only validates that 'status' field is known; it does NOT validate the shape of host_capabilities. A malformed or version-drifted host_capabilities object persists unchanged and could cause downstream errors if a later step attempts to read specific fields (e.g., max_concurrent expecting a number but receiving a string). The C1 protocol says 'explicitly-supplied fields only' and 'omitted field never clobbers,' but there is no Zod schema enforcing the shape of supplied fields (no rejection of unexpected keys, no type coercion). Counterexample: A future version of the host protocol may introduce a new field max_dispatch_latency_ms; an old remediator reading state persisted by a new host would not see it (fields are opaque), but a new remediator reading state persisted by an old host would lack it. If the code ever assumes `state.host_capabilities!.max_concurrent`, it would execute without error on stale state and silently default to undefined behavior (the contract is 'present-but-unvalidated'). No per-field validation schema exists to guard this contract.

findings.title: Host-delegation dispatch obligation has no liveness/progress marker
findings.summary: executeAndRecord writes a `steps/deterministic-progress.json` started/running/complete heartbeat around every deterministic executor dispatch (explicitly to let a host watching the filesystem tell which executor is active during a long-running step, per OBS-0d4c2311). runHostDelegationObligation — the branch that drives the potentially long-running host-delegation / in-process rolling / NIM hybrid-partition dispatch (materializeReviewRun, driveRollingAuditDispatch, driveWithNoProgressRetry) — never touches that marker, so a host relying on it for liveness during the review-dispatch obligation sees no started_at/status update for the whole duration of that step.

async function runHostDelegationObligation(

evidence: src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1418-1424 - executeAndRecord writes steps/deterministic-progress.json with status:"running"/started_at before every deterministic executor dispatch, and status:"complete" after.

src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:2098-2399 - runHostDelegationObligation (the audit_tasks_completed obligation's execute) spans ~300 lines calling materializeReviewRun, driveWithNoProgressRetry/driveRollingAuditDispatch, planHybridDispatch and ensureSemanticReviewRun, none of which write to deterministic-progress.json.
src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts (whole file) - a project-wide search for "deterministic-progress" only matches inside executeAndRecord; no other write site exists for this marker.

findings.title: Linked smoke's present_report handling lacks the friction-triage attestation loop its packaged twin has, so it asserts status 'complete' on a step the CLI emits as 'ready'
findings.summary: The audit orchestrator folds a mandatory friction-triage close-out into the terminal present_report step (it pauses at status 'ready' until the host disposes friction categories — src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts documents this and smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs handles it by writing category_attestations). smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs's nextStepUntilPresentReport returns the first present_report step unconditionally and then asserts `presented.status === "complete"`, so a linked smoke run hits the ready-state friction pause and fails the assertion. The two smokes are near-identical ~900-line copies that have already drifted on this behavior.

affected_files.quoted_text: assert.equal(presented.status, "complete");

evidence: scripts/audit/smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs:174-186 - nextStepUntilPresentReport returns the first present_report step with no friction_record handling.

scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs:211-231 - the packaged twin explicitly handles 'Mandatory friction triage: present_report pauses at status:"ready" until the host covers every friction category' by writing category_attestations and looping.

src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts:171-177 - 'Terminal friction-TRIAGE close-out for audit. Folded into the `present_report` terminal step ... Blocks ("dispose") until every mechanical event + reflection is disposed', confirming the gate applies to any next-step driver, linked or packaged.

findings.title: Malformed or missing findings array silently scores as "zero findings"
findings.summary: cmdScoreAudit reads audit-findings.json and, if the `findings` field is absent or not an array, silently substitutes an empty array instead of failing loud. A truncated or malformed findings file therefore scores as a clean run with zero findings and no diagnostic, which can let a broken input pass the hallucination-rate regression gate that is the command's entire purpose.

affected_files.quoted_text: const findings = Array.isArray(findingsDoc.findings) ? findingsDoc.findings : [];

evidence: src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:61 - `findingsDoc.findings` defaults to `[]` with no warning when the field is missing or the wrong shape; there is no console.error/exitCode signal distinguishing 'genuinely zero findings' from 'malformed input'.

src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:87-104 - the resulting scorecard (built from the silently-emptied findings) is the sole input to the hallucination-rate gate, so a corrupted findings file can score as a clean pass rather than erroring the way the missing --labels path already does (lines 43-49).

findings.title: Mid-flight merge classifies still-running workers as terminal failures and clears their claims
findings.summary: merge-and-ingest has no barrier against being invoked while dispatched workers (timeout_ms up to 30 min) are still running: a task whose result file is not yet on disk is pushed to `failing` as 'Missing audit result for assigned task', its claim is then unconditionally cleared via claimRegistry.clear(terminalTaskIds), and a retry-dispatch is written — so a slow-but-healthy worker is double-dispatched, and its late result can then be excluded as unowned by the ownership gate after a peer re-claims.

findings.title: No mid-run re-negotiation when host dispatch capability changes
findings.summary: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). findings.title: Parallelize independent test suites across separate vitest processes
findings.summary: vitest.config.ts runs 515 tests (246 audit + 138 remediate + 131 shared) sequentially in a single process. The three test suites are independent (no shared mutable state, distinct setup/teardown, separate fixture dirs). Parallelize via separate vitest processes or a test orchestration layer (e.g., npm scripts running vitest run tests/audit, vitest run tests/remediate, vitest run tests/shared concurrently). Requires verification that fixture dirs and temp state don't conflict, but test isolation appears safe. Estimated wall-time reduction: ~40-50% (from 3×sequential to max(audit_time, remediate_time, shared_time)).

findings.title: Resume transition returns status 'partial', which the module's own contract defines as livelock-terminal
findings.summary: advanceRollingPause returns undefined in TWO distinct cases: the run RESUMED (next.kind === 'running' — pause cleared, no terminal recorded, packets should re-dispatch next pass) and the run went livelock-TERMINAL (partial-completion terminal recorded). driveRollingAuditDispatch collapses both into `status: pausedState ? "paused" : run.status`, so a resume-eligible pass surfaces as status 'partial' with non-empty stranded_ids — a status the DriveRollingAuditDispatchResult doc explicitly defines as 'terminal: the pause limit was reached (livelock) and the stranded packets are yielded to synthesis on partial coverage'. A caller keying handoff rendering or terminal handling off this status will misreport a resumable run as a give-up (no terminal actually exists on disk, so state derivation and the return value disagree).

findings.title: Risk score is an unbounded additive sum hard-clamped to 10, so a third of units tie at the ceiling where ranking matters most
findings.summary: Raised by the mathematician perspective; clamp verified at src/audit/extractors/risk.ts:202 (`Math.min(MAX_RISK_SCORE, riskScore)`, MAX_RISK_SCORE=10). risk_score sums several independently-uncapped terms (unit risk, flow hits, external hits, stateful-behavior bonus, the capped structural family, complexity/duplication bonuses) then applies one hard ceiling; in the current run 8 of 22 scored units (36%) land at exactly 10 despite visibly different signal profiles, collapsing the ranking precisely at the top where dispatch prioritization, review-packet ordering, and human triage need discrimination. The file itself shows the author knows correlated signals need bounding (STRUCTURAL_FAMILY_CAP exists so one family 'cannot ALONE saturate risk_score') — the same discipline was not applied to the outer sum. Low-cost fixes: persist the pre-clamp raw score alongside the clamped one so downstream consumers can break ceiling ties, or rescale weights so 10 is reached only by genuinely maximal units. Note: per DR-001, the member_of_cycle input to this score is currently contaminated — fix that first, then re-evaluate the distribution.

findings.title: acquireLock issues mkdir(recursive) on every acquisition, adding an FS round-trip to the hot admission path
findings.summary: Every lock acquisition unconditionally awaits mkdir(dirname(lockPath), {recursive:true}) before the wx create. The reservation ledger takes this lock for EVERY admit, reconcile, outstanding, snapshot and reclaim call (one admit + one reconcile per dispatched packet), and quota-state mutations ride the same primitive, so a run of N packets pays ~2N+ redundant mkdir syscalls for a directory that exists after the first call. Cheap individually but pure overhead in the per-packet admission loop; a once-per-path memo (or creating on ENOENT only) removes it.

findings.title: checkAffectedFileIntegrity's ENOENT-to-missing branch is unreachable; a file deleted mid-check is misclassified as an io_error
findings.summary: The hash callback catches ENOENT to return { kind: 'missing' }, but hashAffectedPath can never throw: it wraps its stat/hash work in its own try/catch that swallows every error (reportHashIoError + return undefined), and returns undefined for a non-existent path via its own existsSync guard. findings.title: Dated 'sunset cleanup' deprecation has no enforcement and has already lapsed
findings.summary: Raised by the adversary perspective. src/shared/providers/agyProvider.ts falls back from the `agy` CLI to a legacy `gemini` binary with a comment gating it 'for July 18, 2026 sunset cleanup' — a dated commitment with no code that reads a clock, warns, or disables after the date, which has now passed (2026-07-21) with the fallback fully live and no runtime deprecation signal when the gemini path is taken. Small but concrete instance of a class worth guarding: a dated cleanup promise living only in a comment is mechanically indistinguishable from one that will never be honored. Either add a runtime lapsed-date warning/removal, or drop the date; consider a repo-wide lint for dated TODO/sunset markers past their date.

findings.title: Run the two independent postinstall sub-deployers concurrently instead of serially blocking
findings.summary: scripts/postinstall.mjs deploys BOTH host asset sets (audit-code and remediate-code) by looping over ["./audit/postinstall.mjs", "./remediate/postinstall.mjs"] and calling `spawnSync` on each in turn — the second sub-deployer does not start until the first has fully exited, even though the script's own comment says each sub-deployer is 'self-contained' and they are run 'as isolated child processes so one host's deployment never corrupts the other's.' That isolation rationale argues FOR concurrency, not against it — nothing links the two runs except the shared parent waiting on them one at a time. Since they are already isolated, independent child processes with no shared mutable state (this is exactly the 'serial that could be parallel' case), replace the sequential spawnSync loop with a concurrent `spawn` + `Promise.all` (or `child_process.fork`/async spawnSync-equivalent) so both host installs run in parallel and postinstall wall time drops to max(audit_install, remediate_install) instead of their sum, directly speeding up every `npm install` of the package. This is a distinct improvement from the already-banked postinstall-duplication finding, which targets code-structure duplication between scripts/audit/postinstall.mjs and scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs, not the root orchestrator's serial execution model.

findings.summary: The mcp inline path constructs a file URL as 'file:///' + path with backslashes swapped. On POSIX (absolute paths start with '/') this yields file:////... findings.title: Sticky failure cache: one transient read failure permanently forfeits the statics dataset for the process lifetime, silently

findings.title: writeManagedMarkdown rewrites the entire user file's line endings, not just the managed block
findings.summary: upsertManagedBlock normalizes the WHOLE existing file to LF and returns that normalized content (with the block substituted), and writeManagedMarkdown writes it back. The managed-block targets are user-owned files (AGENTS.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md), so on a Windows/CRLF repo every install/ensure run converts the user's entire file to LF — a content change well outside the audit-tools-owned marker region, producing noisy whole-file diffs. Both the audit and remediate io modules share the behavior.
evidence: wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-io.mjs:128 - the entire existing content is CRLF-to-LF normalized before the block substitution, and the normalized whole-file string is what gets returned and written.
wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-install-hosts.mjs:533-546 - the targets are AGENTS.md and .github/copilot-instructions.md, user-owned files where content outside the marker block belongs to the user.

findings.title: buildTaskSections silently defaults a missing total_lines to 0 instead of failing loud
findings.summary: coverageTemplate in buildTaskSections falls back to a bare 0 when a task's own file_line_counts is missing the path AND the aggregate lineIndex also misses it (e.g. a synthetic follow-up/deepening task without file_line_counts, for a file no other pending task currently covers). The emitted packet prompt explicitly warns the worker that a zero/omitted total_lines 'will cause fatal validation errors', yet the tool itself can embed a silent 0 into the copy-exactly coverage template with no warning surfaced, contradicting its own documented contract. The sibling extractPacketAnchor function in the same file avoids this by falling back to the packet's own total_lines instead of 0, showing the safer pattern already exists nearby but isn't reused here.

findings.title: cancel-in-progress on main pushes leaves intermediate main commits without a completed suite run
findings.summary: The concurrency group is keyed on github.ref with cancel-in-progress: true, so on branch main two pushes in quick succession cancel the earlier commit's in-flight matrix. Combined with the release-bump skip, stretches of main history can carry no completed orchestration-suite evidence, which weakens bisection and the 'green main' signal. This is a common intentional trade for PR branches; on the trunk it silently drops coverage of superseded commits.

affected_files.quoted_text: cancel-in-progress: true

evidence: .github/workflows/audit-code-test-suite.yml:41-43 - `group: audit-code-test-suite-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}` with cancel-in-progress applies to refs/heads/main pushes as well as PRs, so a newer main push cancels the previous main commit's run.

findings.title: claude-worker silently substitutes the dummy auth token when the configured api_key_env variable is unset or blank
findings.summary: When config.api_key_env names an env var that is unset or empty at launch, the provider falls back to CLAUDE_WORKER_DUMMY_API_KEY with no warning. Against a keyed proxy every spawned worker then fails auth, and the failure surfaces only as opaque per-packet worker errors — there is no diagnostic tying the failures to the missing credential, so the misconfiguration is expensive to trace.
evidence: src/shared/providers/claudeWorkerProvider.ts:175 - the `|| CLAUDE_WORKER_DUMMY_API_KEY` fallback applies both to the intentionally-keyless case (api_key_env absent) and to the misconfigured case (api_key_env set but the variable empty/unset), with no distinguishing diagnostic for the latter.

findings.title: ClaudeWorkerProvider's per-launch config-dir cleanup failure is swallowed with zero diagnostic signal
findings.summary: The finally-block cleanup of the per-launch isolated CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR uses `.catch(() => {})`, discarding the error entirely (no stderr write, no RunLogger event). If cleanup keeps failing (e.g. AV lock, permissions), temp dirs under os.tmpdir()/audit-tools-claude-worker accumulate on every dispatch with no observable trace pointing at the cause, unlike comparable best-effort paths elsewhere in the packet (e.g. spawnLoggedCommand's onLogStreamError, which annotates and surfaces stream failures).
evidence: src/shared/providers/claudeWorkerProvider.ts:196-200 - rm(...).catch(() => {}) discards the error with no stderr write and no RunLogger event, so a persistently failing cleanup is invisible to the operator

src/shared/providers/spawnLoggedCommand.ts:148-163 - the comparable best-effort log-stream failure path in the same packet DOES annotate and surface its error (onLogStreamError), showing the established pattern this cleanup path departs from

findings.title: Concurrency-peak assertions rely on a fixed 5ms setTimeout to force interleaving instead of a deterministic synchronization signal
findings.summary: Both rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts and rolling-scheduler.test.ts measure 'peak in-flight concurrency' / 'max concurrent rebuilds' by having each dispatched node/rebuild `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 5))` before completing, then reading a counter. This assumes every peer node in a sub-wave is admitted and starts incrementing its counter within that 5ms window before the first one resolves; on a loaded CI runner or a slow machine a scheduler that starts nodes with any meaningful stagger could make the peak assertion (e.g. `expect(peak).toBe(3)`) intermittently under-report, since the test infers concurrency from wall-clock timing rather than an explicit admission barrier.

affected_files.quoted_text: await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 5));
await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 5));

evidence: tests/remediate/rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts:206 - the run() helper increments/decrements `inFlight` around a 5ms sleep and the test then asserts `expect(peak).toBe(3)` for 3 disjoint-file nodes
tests/remediate/rolling-scheduler.test.ts:450 - the single-flight rebuild test increments `concurrentRebuilds` around the same 5ms sleep pattern and asserts `expect(maxConcurrentRebuilds).toBe(1)`

findings.title: Consolidate CLI command dispatch pattern
findings.summary: src/audit/cli/ contains 49 command files (advanceAuditCommand, analyzerInstallCommand, ...). Each typically follows a pattern: parse args → load artifact bundle → execute logic → write step. The command dispatch in cli.ts (the entry point) maps command names to these files. Consider: (1) parameterizing the command registration so new commands don't require a new file + dispatch entry; (2) extracting a `CommandContext` type that all commands receive (artifacts, config, etc.) to reduce boilerplate; (3) consolidating logically-grouped commands (all validation commands, all synthesis commands) into shared modules with sub-dispatch. Not urgent, but high-friction on adding new commands.
evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

findings.title: Corrupt active-dispatch.json is silently treated as absent, dropping resumable pause state
findings.summary: readActiveDispatch (and the parallel inline read in dispatch.ts) swallows ALL read errors via .catch(() => null), including the 'Invalid JSON' error readJsonFile throws on a corrupt file. A corrupt active-dispatch.json is indistinguishable from an absent one: persistPausedState silently no-ops, the carried paused_state (pause_count, settled_exclusions) is lost, and the livelock counter restarts from zero — extending a stall the livelock bound exists to end, with no diagnostic anywhere.

affected_files.quoted_text: const existing = await readJsonFile<ActiveDispatchState>(path).catch(() => null);
const priorActiveDispatch = await readJsonFile<ActiveDispatchState>(

evidence: src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:29 - .catch(() => null) collapses ENOENT, JSON-parse failure, and IO errors into the same 'no artifact' answer; persistPausedState:40 then returns without persisting anything.
src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:679-681 - the carried-pause read uses the same catch-all, so a corrupt artifact resets carriedPausedState/confirmation_shown for the run with no warning.
src/shared/io/json.ts:124-128 - readJsonFile throws a distinct 'Invalid JSON in <path>' error, so corruption is detectable and could be surfaced instead of swallowed.

findings.title: Cycle-guard fixture bakes in a hand-derived internal counting formula
findings.summary: makeCycleTrigger()'s docstring hand-derives the exact internal arithmetic the finalization cycle guard uses to decide when to fire ('index + 1 - seenStateSignatures.size >= tolerance') and bakes that formula into the fixture instead of driving the guard to trip through its public/observable behavior. If the internal accounting is ever refactored without changing observable behavior, this fixture's hand-derived index/seed values can silently stop tripping the guard (a false negative) rather than the test failing loudly.
evidence: tests/audit/finalization-cycle-guard.test.mjs:100-121 - the fixture builder's comment shows manual algebra re-deriving the exact trip condition of checkFinalizationCycle, which the test then hand-encodes as index/seenStateSignatures values rather than driving the guard through repeated calls until it observably fires.

findings.title: Directory-hash I/O errors are reported at the top-level path, never the actual failing child file
findings.summary: hashDirectorySync/hashDirectory's recursive walk reads each child file with no per-file try/catch of its own; a mid-walk failure (permission denied, file removed) propagates up and is only caught in hashAffectedPathSync/hashAffectedPath, which logs via reportHashIoError(absolutePath, err) using the top-level directory path passed in, not the specific child file that actually failed — so the emitted file_integrity_io_error diagnostic never identifies which file inside the directory caused the failure.

affected_files.quoted_text: const content = readFileSync(child);
reportHashIoError(absolutePath, err);

evidence: src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:71-83 - hashDirectorySync's inner visit() calls readFileSync(child) directly with no local try/catch, so a failure on any nested file throws out of the whole walk.

src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:120-128 - the exception is only caught at the outer hashAffectedPathSync, which reports it via reportHashIoError(absolutePath, err) — absolutePath here is the top-level directory being hashed, not the nested child path where the read actually failed, so the structured stderr diagnostic loses the file-level detail reportHashIoError is designed to carry.
src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:89-108 - the async hashDirectory has the identical gap (readFile(child) inside visit with no local catch, caught only at hashAffectedPath's outer try).
runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

findings.title: dispatch-status undercounts total/completed tasks whose results predate the current run
findings.summary: cmdDispatchStatus skips every dispatch-result-map entry whose packet_id is '__prior_dispatch__' before building packetStatus, so those tasks (results that already existed on disk before this run started) never contribute to total_tasks or completed_tasks — the reported progress undercounts true scope on any resumed run.

affected_files.quoted_text: if (pid === "__prior_dispatch__") continue;
const totalTasks = packetStatus.reduce((s, p) => s + p.task_count, 0);

evidence: src/audit/cli/dispatchStatusCommand.ts:40 - entries with packet_id '__prior_dispatch__' are skipped and never pushed into packetStatus.

src/audit/cli/dispatchStatusCommand.ts:63-64 - totalTasks/completedTasks are derived purely from packetStatus, so any task whose result predates this run (see dispatch.ts's priorResultTaskIds -> '__prior_dispatch__' entries) is silently excluded from both the numerator and denominator, misreporting how much of the run's real task set is actually done.

findings.title: Enable TypeScript incremental compilation so the commit-gate typecheck and the build compile don't each re-check the whole program cold
findings.summary: tsconfig.base.json (extended by tsconfig.json) sets strict/target/module options but no `incremental`/`tsBuildInfoFile`. Two separate full-program tsc invocations exist over the same `src` tree with nothing shared between them: `npm run build` (`tsc -p tsconfig.json`, emits dist/) and `npm run check` (`tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit`), the latter run on EVERY commit by the PreToolUse hook (.claude/hooks/pre-commit-gate.mjs, `execSync('npm run check', ...)`, materialized against a freshly checked-out scratch git index each time — a cold worktree with no carried-over build state). Because neither invocation sets `incremental`, every `npm run check` re-type-checks the entire program from scratch even when only a handful of files changed since the last commit's `build`/`check` pass, and `npm run build` (invoked by `npm test`, `verify:checks`, and every release) likewise starts cold every time. TypeScript's incremental mode exists exactly for this repeated-full-recompile pattern: adding `"incremental": true` plus a `tsBuildInfoFile` (e.g. `.tsbuildinfo`, gitignored) lets a second tsc invocation over largely-unchanged source reuse the cached program/type-check state and only re-verify what changed, instead of a full cold pass. This doesn't remove a step (build and check remain distinct, intentional gates per CLAUDE.md's green-at-every-commit invariant) but cuts the wall-clock cost of the pattern CLAUDE.md's own pipeline-profiling section already names as a tracked cost ('the check/build double-tsc... timed') — this finding is the concrete fix (incremental caching) for a cost the profiling infra measures but nothing yet addresses.

findings.title: foldAccountCooldown is documented 'Pure' but fabricates updated_at from the ambient clock for synthesized entries
findings.summary: foldAccountCooldown's docstring claims 'Pure; never mutates its inputs', but when ownEntry is null and a sibling contributes a cooldown signal it synthesizes an entry stamped `updated_at: new Date().toISOString()` — a nondeterministic ambient-clock read. The fabricated timestamp marks data that was actually learned earlier (from a sibling's entry) as freshly captured; QuotaStateEntry.updated_at is consumed as a capture/freshness timestamp elsewhere (learnedQuotaSource maps it to captured_at), so a folded entry can masquerade stale sibling state as fresh. It also makes the fold non-referentially-transparent, defeating the identity comparison at apiPool.foldAccountCooldownAcrossPools (`folded === (pool.quotaStateEntry ?? null)`) which can only ever be true in the all-null case.

findings.title: Job-level timeout is smaller than the sum of its own step timeouts
findings.summary: The gate and test jobs each declare timeout-minutes: 20 at the job level, but their own step-level timeout-minutes budgets sum to more (gate: 10+15=25; test: 10+10+15=35, plus checkout/setup overhead in both). A run that legitimately uses most of a step's declared budget will instead be killed by the enclosing job timeout before that step's own timeout (and its 'trap ERR' diagnostic message) ever fires, so the failure surfaces as a generic runner timeout instead of the intended '::error::' attribution naming which step stalled.

runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20

- name: Install dependencies
        timeout-minutes: 10

- name: Verify release checks (non-test gate)
        timeout-minutes: 15

runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 20

- name: Build
        timeout-minutes: 10

- name: Run tests (shard ${{ matrix.shard }}/4)
        timeout-minutes: 15

findings.title: mergeGitignoreBlock leaves orphaned markers in place on degenerate marker states, causing duplicate managed blocks to accumulate
findings.summary: The replace-in-place path requires begin AND end markers with endIdx > beginIdx. If a .gitignore holds only one marker, or the END marker precedes BEGIN (e.g. a hand-edited or corrupted prior block), the function falls through to the append branch WITHOUT removing the stale marker lines. On the next run the stale marker pairs with the new block's marker (indexOf finds the first occurrence), so the 'replaced' span can clobber user lines between the orphaned marker and the new block, or the degenerate ordering persists and a fresh block is appended every run.
evidence: src/shared/io/gitignoreArtifacts.ts:178-182 - only the well-formed BEGIN-before-END case is replaced in place; all degenerate states (lone BEGIN, lone END, END-before-BEGIN) silently take the append path with the stale marker(s) left in the file.

src/shared/io/gitignoreArtifacts.ts:192-194 - the append branch never strips stray marker lines, so a file with a lone orphaned END marker gains a full appended block; a subsequent run then sees beginIdx (new block) > endIdx (orphan) and appends again — the documented idempotence ('re-run ... yields byte-identical output') does not hold for these states.

findings.title: parseResetValue returns unparseable raw text as a reset timestamp
findings.summary: When a reset header value is neither ISO-shaped nor a positive number, parseResetValue falls through to `return trimmed;` rather than null, so ExtractedRateLimits.reset_at can carry an arbitrary non-timestamp string (e.g. a duration like '6m0s' parses to reset_at '6m0s' only when parseFloat yields NaN, e.g. 'in 5 minutes'). Downstream consumers treating reset_at as a timestamp get Invalid Date instead of the documented null degrade.

findings.title: Schema regeneration writes each committed schema file in-place sequentially with no atomicity, so a mid-loop failure leaves the schema set half-updated
findings.summary: The generator overwrites each file under schemas/ one at a time directly at its final path. If rendering or writing a later file throws, earlier files are already replaced, leaving the committed generated-artifact set internally inconsistent (some schemas from the new zod source, some from the old). Git and the drift test bound the blast radius, but the write itself has no temp-then-rename or all-or-nothing step.

findings.title: validateDispatchArtifacts records each result's plan phase but validates every referenced result against the implement-only contract
findings.summary: The referencedResults map is keyed with the plan's phase ('document' | 'implement'), but the phase value is never read when validating: every existing referenced result file is passed to validateImplementWorkerResult, which requires phase === 'implement' and implement-shaped item_results. validateDispatchPlan still accepts phase 'document' as valid, so a legacy document-phase run's result files produce false 'phase must be implement' validation errors during the sweep. Either document results need their own validator selected by the stored phase, or the stored phase is dead data and 'document' should no longer validate as an acceptable phase.

findings.title: Wall-stranded task claims stay held for the full 20-minute lease while the run is paused
findings.summary: When the host-path quota wall fires, prepareDispatchArtifacts releases the admission LEASES (reconcileAdmissionLeasesFromQuotaFile) but never clears the cooperative per-task CLAIMS it took via taskClaims.claimMany, even though nothing will be dispatched this turn. The code explicitly releases claims for budget-deferred tasks because 'holding them would hoard deferred work a peer could otherwise pick up' — the identical rationale applies to wall-stranded emitted tasks (a peer IDE on a different quota pool is not walled), and re-grant is idempotent for the same runId so releasing would not break resume. Result: up to AUDIT_TASK_CLAIM_LEASE_MS (20 min) of peer-visible hoarding per wall pass.

affected_files.quoted_text: if (deferredClaimed.length > 0) await taskClaims.clear(deferredClaimed);
await reconcileAdmissionLeasesFromQuotaFile(dispatchQuotaPath);

evidence: src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:452-459 - deferred-task claims are released with the anti-hoarding rationale in the adjacent comment.
src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:755-775 - the wall.atWall branch releases leases and records the pause but performs no taskClaims.clear for the emitted (now stranded) task set.
src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:128-135 - AUDIT_TASK_CLAIM_LEASE_MS = 20 * 60_000 bounds how long a peer is locked out of the stranded tasks.

findings.title: 'One user, no external consumers' design license sits alongside a publicly published npm package
findings.summary: Raised by the pragmatist perspective; kept as a lone but genuine framing observation (low confidence acknowledged). CLAUDE.md justifies unlimited complexity budget on 'one user, no external consumers,' while package.json publishes audit-tools publicly with discovery keywords and README onboards an unknown installer. If the honest audience is one operator, much of the everything-agnostic machinery (DR-006) is speculative; if it is the public npm audience the packaging implies, the 'complexity is free' license is the wrong governing assumption, because external users pay full price for internal-only conveniences (58-command CLI, 9-provider matrix, 24-obligation pipeline). The load-bearing assumption should be stated explicitly, since the two audiences justify materially different amounts of the ceremony flagged in DR-005/DR-006/DR-015.

findings.title: worker-schema drift test is a sync guard over committed generated artifacts — the duplication it polices (committed schemas vs. zod sources) is the underlying cost
findings.summary: generate-schemas.mjs's own header documents that tests/audit/worker-schema-generation.test.mjs exists to fail whenever the committed schemas/ JSON drifts from what the zod sources would render. That test is a drift guard: it keeps two representations of one contract (zod source and committed JSON schemas) in sync by assertion rather than by construction. Generating the schemas at build time into dist/ (they are already regenerable on demand) would remove the second copy and make the guard unnecessary; if committing generated JSON is a deliberate shipping requirement, the guard is the accepted workaround and this is informational.
evidence: scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:5-8 - the header names the drift test and states its purpose is to detect committed-file drift from the zod sources, i.e. a sync test policing a duplicated contract representation.

themes.title: Ingest trusts what is on disk, not what was assigned
themes.root_cause: Every merge/ingest surface derives completion and identity from the result files that happen to exist rather than reconciling against the pending-task manifest and a runtime-validated contract: missing results read as done, support JSON reads as results, unknown task_ids validate against an empty manifest, and worker outcome enums are never schema-checked at the boundary.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Make ingestion manifest-first: load pending-audit-tasks.json as the authority, hard-fail on unaccounted tasks, filter known support filenames, validate every result (including enum fields) through the shared Zod contract before any merge, and count completion only from reconciled task ids.

themes.title: Cross-process coordination is lease-based but liveness-blind
themes.root_cause: Claims and file locks share one design gap: possession is judged by timestamps and fail-open reads, never by holder liveness or write fencing. A dead round's claims strand work and livelock the drain; a slow live holder gets its lock stolen and later blind-overwrites the stealer; torn registries read as unclaimed; ownership checkpoints drop in-flight claims on restart.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Fence every guarded write with a version/token compare, heartbeat long-held locks (or import a vetted lock library per the two-tier dependency policy), quarantine-and-halt on torn claim state instead of failing open, serialize in-flight claims into checkpoints, and give dispatch rounds a terminal path that releases claims when workers die without results.

themes.title: Planned work and declared states that can never happen
themes.root_cause: Several orchestrator outputs are computed and documented but never persisted or reachable: requeue coverage tasks are folded into metrics yet never into the dispatched task list; the blocked status exists in the type but no obligation ever produces it; the friction-triage step pauses without authorizing a continuation command.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Close the loop between computation and consumption: persist exactly the artifact the downstream reader consumes (assert it in a test), and delete or wire dead states so the type system reflects reachable reality.

themes.title: The dedupe engine can silently lose findings
themes.root_cause: crossLensDedupe/sameLensDedupe keep scanning from an absorbed i-slot survivor and mis-key clone-mode identity, so later findings can merge into removed ghosts or resurrect absorbed originals — and the entire family has near-zero direct test coverage, so the loss is invisible.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Restart or re-anchor the scan when the i-slot is absorbed, key clone bookkeeping by stable identity rather than object reference, and add three-or-more-finding merge tests asserting one survivor with the union of evidence.

themes.title: Phantom graph edges corrupt the architecture signal chain
themes.root_cause: Cycle detection folds non-import edge kinds (repo-path prose references, heuristics) into the import relation, so the flagship multi-module dependency-cycle findings and the member_of_cycle risk score ride on edges with no load-order semantics.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Restrict cycle detection to structural import/call edges via an explicit edge-kind selector; surface reference/heuristic edges as separately-labeled signals that never feed cycle or risk scoring.

themes.title: One core, two draws — violated at the largest surfaces
themes.root_cause: The audit/remediate split re-implements shared machinery per side: ~2500 lines of near-identical wrapper/ bootstrap code, mirrored provider directories, four copy-pasted smoke harnesses, five hand-rolled remediate gates that duplicate audit's generic gate engine inside a 5,209-line nextStep.ts, and a hand-rolled audit CLI parser beside remediate's commander adoption.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Promote the duplicated core to audit-tools/shared as parameterized factories (wrapper core, provider wrappers, gate engine, smoke harness), decompose the nextStep monolith along phase seams, and adopt vetted libraries (execa, commander) for lifecycle plumbing the project does not need to own.

themes.title: The test suite can pass without testing
themes.root_cause: A cluster of suite-integrity defects lets regressions through: dist/ imports that silently exercise stale builds (including one that evades the repo's own guard), tests that return early without assertions, an afterEach that hangs the suite, drift-guard tests that hand-duplicate the constants they police, and pinned fixtures that certify unusable or malformed behavior.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Import from source everywhere (and widen the dist-import guard to path.join constructions), ban assertion-free early returns via a lint, extract shared fixtures/constants so drift guards become unnecessary, and make negative-path fixtures mirror real tool output.

themes.title: Failures swallowed into false green
themes.root_cause: Multiple terminal paths report success while discarding evidence of failure: signal-killed release-gate steps map to exit 0, final-state persistence failures are silently dropped before artifact cleanup, skipped closing actions count as passed, rollback commands are unchecked, and builds succeed without required assets.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Treat every non-success terminal (signal, skipped, swallowed catch) as an explicit reported outcome: inspect result.signal, log-and-event persistence failures, make verification verdicts consume closing/rollback results, and write snapshots atomically.

themes.title: Dispatch routing economics and capability resolution mis-rank backends
themes.root_cause: Routing decisions consume wrong or unvalidated inputs: per-token advert prices land in the per-Mtok field (1,000,000x underestimate), auto-resolution selects a provider whose launch always rejects headless mode, all-oversized work dereferences an empty allocation, and exhaustion has no cross-provider fallback.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Normalize units at the adapter boundary with a typed conversion, compose capability predicates (headless-capable x configured x available) into resolution, return a structural no-capable-pool outcome instead of an empty primary, and add a declared fallback chain consumed by the rolling engine.

themes.title: Remediate gate and state-machine integrity gaps
themes.root_cause: The remediation state machine trusts artifacts it never correlates and transitions it never re-checks: review resolutions apply without plan-id correlation, autonomous leftovers replay as approved, partial triage closes runs with blocked items, block splits drop verification metadata, malformed incoming submissions crash-loop the generic gate engine, and friction run-ids double-sanitize into unreadable paths.
themes.suggested_fix_pattern: Correlate every applied artifact to its emitting run (run-unique ids checked at apply), guard each state transition against a single allowed-transition table, generalize the quarantine helper into runOmittableGate so all gates inherit it, and single-source sanitization at one boundary.

executive_summary: The self-audit surfaced 1480 findings whose high-severity core clusters into a few systemic causes rather than scattered bugs: ingestion and merge paths that trust on-disk presence over the assigned manifest, lease-based claims and locks with no liveness or write fencing (observed live as a drain livelock that generated 571 empty run dirs), planned work that is computed but never persisted for dispatch, and a finding-dedupe engine that can silently lose results. A second band of risk is meta: the test suite itself can pass without testing (stale-dist imports, assertion-free early returns, drift guards duplicating what they police), and several terminal paths convert failures into false green. The architecture signal chain is additionally contaminated by non-import edges feeding cycle detection, so the largest reported cycles are phantoms. Remediation should start where corruption is silent — ingest reconciliation, claim/lock fencing, dedupe correctness — then burn down the duplication between the two orchestrators that multiplies every future fix.
top_risks: Silent loss of audit results: manifest-blind merging plus the dedupe absorbed-survivor bug can drop findings end-to-end with no error (COR-7602834d, COR-5c71a9ff).
Concurrent-run state corruption: liveness-blind lock stealing and fail-open claim reads permit double-writers on state, quota, and claims files (REL-d3d6f01b-3, COR-888321f5).

Autonomy stalls and livelocks: merge-only claim release plus zero-grant drain rounds strand 20-minute leases and burn maxTransitions when workers die (COR-6ba55c63); malformed incoming JSON crash-loops the gate engine (REL-b38f2c8a).
Mandatory coverage gaps never audited: requeue tasks are built but never dispatched, so promised lens coverage silently does not happen (COR-58ccee39).
False-green release and close-out signals: signal-killed gate steps and skipped closing actions report success (COR-85a995a0-2, COR-fb656e3f-2).
Mis-ranked dispatch economics: the 1,000,000x price-unit error can route paid traffic as if free (COR-6c1ad0ba).
Suite integrity: stale-dist imports and assertion-free tests mean green CI does not certify current source behavior (TST-ca020ddd, TST-671ac34d).
